474.name: Bone mineral density
474.description: Bone mineral density (BMD) refers to the amount of minerals, mainly calcium and phosphorus, present in the bones. An adequate BMD level is essential to maintain strong and resistant bones.

477.name: Systolic blood pressure levels
477.description: Systolic blood pressure is the pressure exerted by the blood ejected by ventricular systole against the arterial wall. The study of blood pressure levels is of utmost importance, especially when an increase is detected, as this constitutes a significant risk factor for the development of cardiovascular diseases.

544.name: Apolipoprotein A1 levels
544.description: Apolipoprotein A1 or ApoA1 is a key protein in lipid metabolism, is the main protein component of high-density lipoproteins (HDL) or "good cholesterol" and is involved in the transport of cholesterol from the tissues to the liver for excretion, among other processes.

421.name: Prediction of visceral adipose tissue
421.description: Visceral adipose tissue is the fat stored around internal organs. Its increase has been linked as a risk factor for cardiovascular diseases, such as heart attack and atherosclerosis, and metabolic diseases.
421.overview:
Adipose tissue is an important site for lipid storage, energy homeostasis, and whole-body insulin sensitivity. There are two distinct classifications of adipose tissue: white adipose tissue and brown adipose tissue. White adipose tissue accounts for the majority of fat present in adult humans and brown adipose tissue predominate in postnatal babies.  The formation of adipose tissue or adipogenesis is the development of fully differentiated mature adipocytes from precursor cells.

Adipogenesis occurs in two stages, first it is produced the determination of an indiferenciated cell into a preadipocyte and at a second stage the preadipocyte becomes a mature adipocyte. This process is regulated by hormones and transcription factors.

Adipocyte differentiation is closely regulated by a cascade of transcription factors that interact with the DNA and promote the expression of genes that determine the features of a mature adipocyte, amongst them the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPARG).
421.technical_description:
Persistent energy imbalance caused by an excessive caloric intake leads to obesity and, often, to associated metabolic disorders. The most typical manifestation is the resistance to the action of insulin which in advanced stages leads to type 2 diabetes.

PPARG2 is one of the most studied genes as potentially linked to obesity phenotypes, increased adiposity and insulin resistance. PPARG2 gene encodes for the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma 2 (PPARG2) which is a transcription that binds to their target genes involved in the adipogenesis or adipose tissue formation (Chung et al. 2016). Loss of PPARG2 activity leads to a loss of adipose tissue in mouse model and reduce the human adipocyte differentiation (Barak et al. 1999; Majithia et al. 2014).

The single nucleotide polimorphysm (SNP) rs1801282, p.Pro12Ala or P12A was the first mutation identified in PPARG2 that results in C→G substitution and the conversion of proline to alanine at residue 12 of the PPARG2 protein. This change in the protein cause moderate decrease in its DNA-binding affinity function that leads to a reduction in transcription activity.

The p.Pro12Ala polymorphism has been associated with obesity and a decrease in the risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease in Caucasians. This variant is a common variant, but the effect on type 2 diabetes risk is modest (20-25% decrease). It should be noted that this effect may vary depending on the population, the 12Ala allele does not reduce the risk of diabetes in South Asians, Chinese, and Malaysians (Chung et al. 2016). 

There are studies that suggest that Ala12 allele has a protective role against insulin resistance and against weight gain in a high monounsaturated fatty accid (MUFA) diet. The physiological explanation for this phenomenon could be related to the fact that the Ala12 variant is associated with a reduced transcriptional activity and a reduced DNA-binding affinity and a reduced activation of adipogenesis (Garaulet et al. 2011).

The body mass index or BMI and the ratio of fat to saturated fat (P:S ratio) has been correlated to p.Pro12Ala presence. It has been shown that Ala12 carriers were more obese or has a BMI greater than Pro12 homozygotes when P:S ratio is low. In contrast, Ala12 carriers (has a rs1801282-G allele) are less obese than Pro12 carriers when de P:S ratio in high. Because of that it is considered that Ala12 are more sensitive to diets rich in saturated fats and simple sugars by inducing hyperplasia (proliferating precursor cells) and hypertrophy of adipose tissue (triglyceride accumulation in preadipocytes). While diets high in PUFAs, especially diets rich in n-3 fatty acids, prevent adipogenesis. Individuals with the Pro12Pro genotype are more likely to benefit from polyunsaturated fatty acids containing diet (Chung et al. 2016; Galbete et al. 2013; Robitaille et al. 2003).

It remains unclear whether PPARG2 influences weight reduction in response to a dietary treatment and there are differences due to ethnic heterogeneity. Some publications show that Ala12 allele carriers are more prone to weight loss than Pro12Pro carriers when exposed to a comprehensive lifestyle intervention, but they appear less likely to retain these benefits when returning to the habitual diet, as they regain more weight than Pro12 homozygotes (Lapice and Vaccaro 2014).

476.name: Glycated hemoglobin levels
476.description: Hemoglobin is a protein found in red blood cells or erythrocytes, and when bound to glucose molecules, it gives rise to the glycosylated form of hemoglobin, known as HbA1c. Measurement of HbA1c levels plays a key role in the diagnosis and monitoring of diabetes, a disease that affects about 9.3% of the world`s population.

239.name: LDL cholesterol levels
239.description: LDL cholesterol, commonly called "bad cholesterol," is a fat that circulates in the blood, moving cholesterol through the body to where it is needed for cell repair and depositing it on the inside of artery walls.
239.overview:
Cholesterol is carried in the blood from the intestine or liver to the organs that need it by joining up with particles called lipoproteins.

There are two types of lipoproteins:

    Low density (LDL): transports new cholesterol from the liver to all the cells of the organism
    High density (HDL): takes the unused cholesterol and carries it back to the liver to be stored or eliminated as bile

Referring to this interaction, cholesterol can be classified as

    Bad cholesterol: on attaching to the LDL particle, this cholesterol affixes to the artery walls and forms atherosclerotic plaques
    Good cholesterol: on attaching to the HDL particles, this cholesterol transports the excess cholesterol back to the liver to be eliminated

LDL cholesterol or low density lipoprotein is known as the bad cholesterol because it is associated with heart disease. In general, the persons with lower LGL cholesterol levels have a lower risk of a heart attack or stroke. Keeping a LDL level under 100 mg/dL is recommended.

Genetic variations influence the LDL cholesterol levels, as well as lifestyle. LDL levels can be decreased by quitting smoking, losing weight and being physically more active.

LDL cholesterol levels:

    Less than 70 mg/dL = Optional objective if there is a high risk of heart attack or death by heart attack
    Less than 100 mg/dL = Optimum level for those with heart disease or diabetes
    100 - 129 mg/dL = near or above optimum level
    130 - 159 mg/dL = high limit
    160 - 189 mg/dL = high
    190 mg/dL and above = very high
239.technical_description:
LDL or low density lipoprotein, commonly known as "bad" cholesterol, is closely related to cardiovascular diseases. In general, people with low LDL levels have lower risk of heart attack and stroke. Most doctors recommend keeping LDL blood concentration below 100 mg/dL. Both genetic factors and lifestyle, which can include smoking, diet and performing daily exercise, affect the LDL levels.

In one study, researchers analysed the LDL levels in 100,000 individuals, all of European descent. Finding that each rs6511720 (T) copy in the gene was associated with a mean decrease in LDL cholesterol levels of about 7 mg/dL. Another study confirmed the association in the European population and gave evidence of the association between African Americans, Mexican and Hispanics.

In a study HDL cholesterol levels were analysed in individuals, all of European descent. Finding that each rs964184 (G) allele copy near the LDLR gene was associated with a decrease in HDL cholesterol levels 1.5 mg/dL on average. Another study corroborated this association in European population and gave evidence of the association between Americans and Hispanic Mexicans was also found. In this same study, it was determined that individuals carrying the rs646776 (C) polymorphism, located on SORT1 gene, have an average decrease of 5.65 mg/dL in LDL levels.

It was found evidence for a new LDL locus in the chromosome 1p13.3. These findings potentially provide information about the biological mechanisms that underlie the regulation of LDL cholesterol and might help in the discovery of new therapeutic targets for cardiovascular disease.

238.name: HDL cholesterol levels
238.description: HDL cholesterol is a high-density lipoprotein, commonly known as "good cholesterol" because it is responsible for the storage and excretion of cholesterol, which has been linked to a lower risk of heart attack or stroke.
238.overview:
Cholesterol is carried in the blood from the intestine or liver to the organs that need it by joining up with particles called lipoproteins.

There are two types of lipoproteins:

    Low density (LDL): transports new cholesterol from the liver to all the cells of the organism
    High density (HDL): takes the unused cholesterol and carries it back to the liver to be stored or eliminated as bile

Referring to this interaction, cholesterol can be classified as

    Bad cholesterol: on attaching to the LDL particle, this cholesterol affixes to the artery walls and forms atherosclerotic plaques
    Good cholesterol: on attaching to the HDL particles, this cholesterol transports the excess cholesterol back to the liver to be eliminated

HDL cholesterol or lipoprotein is known as the good cholesterol because it seems to diminish the risk of a heart attack or stroke. This means that unlike other cholesterol levels, the higher HDL cholesterol, the better. It has been established that a HDL under 40 mg/dL significantly increases the risks of heart disease.

Genetic variations influence the HDL cholesterol levels, as well as lifestyle. HDL levels can be increased by quitting smoking, losing weight and being physically more active.

HDL cholesterol levels

    Less than 40 mg/dL in adult men = low HDL (greater risk)
    Less than 50 mg/dL in adult women = low HDL (greater risk)
    40 - 59 mg/dL = the higher the better
    60 and over mg/dL = high HDL (low risk)
238.technical_description:
HDL or high-density lipoprotein is the "good" cholesterol that is considered protective against cardiovascular diseases. Genetic variants influence HDL cholesterol levels and lifestyle, such as regular exercise and not smoking can also increase the HDL cholesterol amount.

Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified multiple loci associated with plasma lipid concentrations. Common variants altogether account for <10% of the variation in each trait of lipids. Rare variants with large individual effects may also contribute to the heritability of lipid traits; however, the degree in which the rare variants affect the lipidic phenotypes should be determined.

In a study HDL cholesterol levels were analysed in 99,900 individuals, all of European descent. The researchers found that each rs964184 (G) allele copy near the APOA1 gene was associated with a decrease in HDL cholesterol levels 1.5 mg/dL on average. Another study corroborated this association in the European population and gave evidence of the association between Americans and Hispanic Mexicans was also found.

553.name: Galectin-3 levels
553.description: Galectin-3 levels are a crucial biomarker for various diseases, and its regulation is influenced by a combination of genetic and environmental factors. Understanding these factors may provide a better insight into the pathogenesis of diseases in which galectin-3 is involved and open new avenues for the development of targeted therapies.

242.name: Vitamin D levels
242.description: Vitamin D is an essential nutrient for the maintenance of our health. Its main function is the regulation of calcium and phosphate levels, so it has a nutritional role in the maintenance of healthy bones and teeth.
242.overview:
Vitamin D is an essential nutrient for the maintenance of our health.  It is important to maintain optimal levels of vitamin D at all stages of our lives, as it is an essential nutrient for the development of important functions such as the formation and maintenance of bones, the absorption of other nutrients such as phosphorus and calcium, as well as the proper functioning of the immune system. In addition, vitamin D, together with calcium, contributes to the prevention of osteoporosis, a pathology that causes bones to become thinner and weaker, leading to the appearance of fractures. Finally, vitamin D is also involved in muscle movement and in the transmission of nerve impulses.

90% of the vitamin D in our body comes from sunlight and is generated from the photolysis of 7-dehydrocholesterol in the skin through the action of UVB (ultraviolet rays). In the absence of sun exposure, at least 1000 IU (preferably 2000 IU) of vitamin D/day would be required to meet the needs of an adult. To a lesser extent, vitamin D can also be obtained from food (especially high-fat fish) and through dietary supplements.

The functions of vitamin D include

Maintenance of intracellular and extracellular calcium concentration.
Increases intestinal absorption of calcium and phosphorus
Stimulates bone resorption
Promotes differentiation to osteoclasts
Increases intestinal calcium transporter protein synthesis
Increases renal reabsorption of calcium and phosphorus
Acts on the immune system
Inhibits proliferation and differentiation of keratinocytes in the skin
Reduces plasma renin activity and levels of angiotensin II
242.technical_description:
Vitamin D metabolism

Vitamin D is found in nature in two forms: ergocalciferol or vitamin D2 and cholecalciferol or vitamin D3.

In humans most of the vitamin comes from the cutaneous transformation of 7-dehydrocholesterol to cholecalciferol in the presence of sunlight, during exposure to ultraviolet light of wavelength between 290-315 nm, photons are absorbed by 7-dehydrocholesterol from the membrane of cells of the epidermis and dermis. The absorption of ultraviolet radiation opens the B-ring of 7-dehydrocholesterol, forming precholecalciferol. This substance is unstable and rapidly converts to cholecalciferol. As vitamin D3 is synthesized, it is released into the extracellular space and penetrates the vascular bed of the dermis. Bound to vitamin D transporter protein, cholecalciferol reaches the liver.

In addition to cutaneous synthesis, as indicated at the beginning, vitamin D can also be obtained to a lesser extent from food, both of animal (cholecalciferol) and plant (ergocalciferol) origin. Being fat-soluble substances, they require the presence of bile salts for their absorption. They are absorbed in 80% of the administered dose. Both ergocalciferol and dietary cholecalciferol reach the liver bound to vitamin D transporter protein.

Regardless of whether the vitamin is synthesized in the skin or obtained from food, it requires two hydroxylations to exert its metabolic actions. The first hydroxylation is carried out at the 25th position of the molecule by 25-hydroxylase in the liver. The 25(OH)vitamin D formed passes into the blood, and is bound to the transporter protein reaches the kidney. In the proximal renal tubule, it is hydroxylated in position 1, by 1-hydroxylase, giving rise to the active vitamin: 1,25(OH)2vitamin D or calcitriol. Also in the kidney, 24,25(OH)2vitamin D is produced by the action of 24-hydroxylase. This vitamin is much less active.

Foods rich in vitamin D are salmon, cow's milk, soy beverage and other vitamin D-fortified vegetable beverages, canned tuna in oil, and yogurt, among others.

Once its action has been exerted, vitamin D is inactivated in the liver by glyco- and sulfoconjugation. The elimination of vitamin D occurs almost entirely by the biliary route.

Hepatic production of 25(OH)vitamin D is substrate-dependent and is not hormonally regulated. In contrast, the synthesis of 1,25(OH)2vitamin D is stimulated by parathyroid hormone (PTH), hypocalcemia and hypophosphatemia. In addition, sex hormones, prolactin, growth hormone (GH) and insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) increase renal production of the active metabolite. Hypocalcemia increases PTH-induced production of 1,25(OH)2vitamin D to maintain plasma calcium levels within normality.

Conversely, hypercalcemia reduces the synthesis of the vitamin. In addition, the decrease in phosphatemia increases, and its increase reduces the production of the active vitamin.

As we have seen previously, vitamin D metabolism is conditioned by a series of enzymes, which are encoded by genes such as CYP2R1, CYP27B1, or CYP24A1, and which act at different stages. The DBP protein is also a key element that determines the correct functioning of vitamin D metabolism, being this protein, as mentioned above, responsible for the transport of calcidiol from the liver to the kidney.

The aforementioned genes encoding these enzymes can undergo alterations that can influence the development of these proteins and thus the metabolism itself.

NADSYN1 gene 

The NADSYN1 gene encodes the enzyme NADSYN1, which is one of the enzymes involved in cholesterol synthesis and promotes the production of NAD(P), the main coenzyme necessary for energy production and lipid synthesis. 

In the epidermis, vitamin D is produced from the cholesterol precursor 7-dehydrocholesterol through a non-enzymatic process that depends on ultraviolet light. However, 7-dehydrocholesterol can be reduced to cholesterol by the action of the enzyme 7-DHC reductase (encoded by the DHCR7 gene) which catalyzes the final step of cholesterol synthesis and thus reduces the substrate available for vitamin D3 synthesis and hence the concentration of 25(OH)D in the blood. It is in this action that the reduced form of NAD(P), which in turn depends on the NADSYN1 gene, intervenes. 

This is why several GWAS studies have associated polymorphisms in this gene with variation in 25(OH)D concentrations, the most relevant to date being the rs3829251 and rs12785878 mutations.

CYP2R1 gene 

The CYP2R1 gene has been identified as coding for the enzyme vitamin D 25 hydroxylase, which transforms ergocalciferol (D2) and cholecalciferol (D3) into calcidiol (which is the circulating form of vitamin D) in the liver. Some GWAS and other studies have identified the involvement of the rs2060793 polymorphism in blood calcidiol concentration, as well as the rs1562902, rs10766197, and rs10741657 polymorphisms, studies carried out with German, Caucasian, or Asian subjects.

VDR gene 

The VDR receptor is an intracellular polypeptide belonging to the family of thyroid hormone receptors, which binds calcitriol to give rise to some of the most recognized functions of vitamin D.

The presence of this receptor has been determined in a large number of cells, is found in more than 30 organs and tissues, sites where the active form of the vitamin can act.

The rs731236 polymorphism has a significant association with vitamin D concentration, where the rs731236-GG genotype has been associated with a state of "vitamin D sufficiency", as well as a better response of individuals when they were supplemented with vitamin D, while the AG and AA genotypes have been associated with a greater predisposition to vitamin D insufficiency.

RXRA gene 

After binding of calcitriol, the active form of vitamin D, to the VDR receptor, this complex binds to retinoid X receptors (RXR), forming a heterodimer. This heterodimer influences the control of genes that in turn regulate circulating levels of vitamin D metabolites.

Several studies have identified that the presence of the A allele in the rs9409929 polymorphism has been associated with increased amounts of both calcidiol and calcitriol.

CYP24A1 gene 

The CYP24A1 enzyme produces hydroxylations in calcidiol and calcitriol, limiting the amount of circulating calcitriol (the active form of the vitamin) when it is elevated and converting it into calcitroic acid for its excretion, as well as transforming calcidiol into secondary products outside of metabolism. Polymorphisms such as rs6068816, which affect this gene, have been associated with vitamin D concentration.

DBP gene 

The DBP or GC protein is a key element that determines the correct functioning of vitamin D metabolism, this protein is responsible for the transport of calcidiol from the liver to the kidney, where it continues its metabolism, transforming into calcitriol, the active form of vitamin D, useful for the organism. The rs2282679 polymorphism has been related by several studies with serum vitamin D concentrations. In these studies, the allele associated with lower vitamin D levels is the G allele. Individuals with two copies of the G allele (GG genotype) had less vitamin D in plasma than carriers of a single allele (GT genotype), who, in turn, on average, had lower vitamin D levels than individuals with the TT genotype.

549.name: Creatinine levels
549.description: Creatinine is an organic compound generated from the metabolism of creatine, an important muscle nutrient. It is produced at a constant rate and is excreted in the urine. It is a useful measure to assess renal function.

214.name: Caffeine dependence after prolonged consumption
214.description: Coffee has been one of the most consumed beverages in the world for centuries. Historically, it has been surrounded by popular beliefs, although, at present, there are multiple scientific studies that attribute beneficial and also negative effects to it.
214.overview:
Coffee is one of the beverages that has more very ancient data compiled about it. It has been said to have beneficial effects but also negative ones. Historically, coffee has been at the center of many popular beliefs, not always scientifically proved, which have caused skepticism. Nonetheless, there is scientific evidence that moderate coffee consumption is not only completely safe but also can have health benefits.

Some of the references obtained from relevant research studies are:

    Coffee and bronchial asthma. It has been discovered that caffeine prevents moderate attacks of this respiratory illness. The study carried out in the U.S.A. and in Italy support the theory that drinking coffee improves the ventilation function in induced bronchoconstriction, monitoring that its consumption does not cause over-stimulation to persons susceptible to it.
    Coffee is a good antioxidant. In addition to caffeine, coffee has other components that, the same as in other plants, has antioxidant properties. Flavonoids (derived polyphenolics) provide an important protection against possible illnesses and also act as a defense mechanism against diverse attacks by microorganisms,
    Coffee sharpens vigilance and raises a person´s mood. Numerous studies show that coffee with caffeine stimulates attention and liveliness and betters performance, during the day as well as at night. Similarly, it counteracts the effects of tiredness in drivers.
    Coffee reduced the risk of kidney stones. After numerous studies, scientists concluded that a specific consequence of coffee is the metabolic effect that can reduce the formation of biliary stones since other drinks with a higher or lower caffeine concentration did not show any significant relation. Additionally, it has been proven that drinking coffee increases the elimination of water and minerals through the urine.
    Coffee reduces the risk of colon cancer. After numerous studies, Swedish scientists concluded that coffee consumption seemed to protect against colon cancer, while tea did the same for rectal tumors. A study in Italy reached the same conclusion.
    Caffeine acts against migraine headaches and boosts certain pain-killing medicines. Caffeine does not alter cardiovascular functions nor does it raise blood pressure in healthy persons.
    Caffeine facilitates the digestive process by stimulating gastric secretion and pancreatic exocrine secretion.

Caffeine and sports. Caffeine produces a maximum stimulating effect for 2 - 4 hours after drinking it and:

    It causes an increase in physical performance because the organism uses body fat as a energy source. That is why it should not contain sugar as sugar provokes a raise in blood sugar and insulin which inhibits the use of fat.
    It reduces the sensation of fatigue, increases precision, agility and coordination, and boosts muscular contractibility mobilizing calcium. The International Olympic Committee authorizes a limit of 12 micrograms/ml., about 8 cups (1000 mg. caffeine)

Caffeine content and physiological effects

The amount of caffeine in a cup of coffee can vary greatly depending on the origin or the coffee and it mixture and the elaboration method. Instant or soluble coffee generally has less caffeine than roasted or ground coffee.

Generally, the amount of caffeine consumption is less than 300 mg/day, which is more or less:

    3-4 cups of roasted and ground coffee
    5 cups of instant coffee
    5 cups of tea.

Shortly after drinking a cup of coffee or tea, the caffeine is distributed throughout the body (nervous system, cardiovascular system, respiratory system, etc.). However, the body does not store caffeine so its effects are short-lived. 
 Each person reacts differently to caffeine. For example, caffeine can stay in the body of pregnant women three times longer than normal in adults while smokers eliminate caffeine twice as rapidly as non-smokers.

The body can become accustomed to caffeine and regular users are less sensitive to its stimulating effects than others. In fact, people tend to regulate their coffee consumption depending on their experience - they drink many cups of coffee in the morning for the stimulating effect, and none in the evening hours before bedtime.

Of all of the physiologic effects of coffee the most well known is as a stimulant of the nervous system. One or two cups of coffee can make one feel more awake, alert and able to concentrate. Caffeine has been proven to counteract fatigue and increase performance. Nevertheless, in those persons sensitive to caffeine, it can delay sleep, decrease sleep time and even lessen the quality of sleep. Caffeine also has various effects on the way a person feels, which can go from pleasurable stimulation and a better mood to anxiety, nervousness and irritability. However, these effects are temporary and depend on the amount of caffeine consumed.

Other short-term physiologic effects of caffeine include an increase in blood pressure, of plasma catecholamines, renins and free fatty acids in serum; the production of urine and gastric acids also increases. Those healthy individuals who regularly consume caffeine achieve tolerance and do not have any adverse effects.

The great majority of scientific and epidemiologic data have reached the conclusion that regularly consuming coffee and other drinks with caffeine is not associated with cardiovascular illnesses, fetus damage, benign breast cancer or cancer of any other type.
214.technical_description:
The rs2472297 polymorphism is located between the CYP1A1 and CYP1A2 genes on chromosome 15q24. A meta-analysis of four GWAS studies on coffee consumption, conducted in a total of approximately 8,000 individuals of European descent, found that the presence of the rs2472297 (T) allele was associated with increased caffeine consumption (p = 5.4 x 10e-14).

In one of the referenced studies, researchers assessed daily coffee consumption in more than 10,000 subjects of European ancestry. Individuals carrying the rs2472297 (T) allele were found to be associated with higher daily coffee consumption (0.25 more cups of coffee/day).

The rs6968865 polymorphism is a SNP located near the AHR gene (aryl hydrocarbon receptor gene) on chromosome 7p21. A meta-analysis of four GWAS studies on coffee consumption, conducted in a total of approximately 8,000 individuals of European descent, found that the presence of the rs6968865 (T) allele was associated with increased caffeine consumption (p = 2.3 x 10e-11) of approximately 0.2 cups of coffee/day.

222.name: Blood glucose
222.description: Glucose is the main sugar found in the blood. It is the main source of energy for all cells and its blood levels are finely regulated. Its alteration can lead to pathologies such as diabetes.
222.overview:
Glucose is a monosaccharide, the most basic unit of carbohydrates.

The human body cannot create glucose on its own; it enters the organism through food intake. In necessary or urgent situations, the body obtains glucose from the transformation of fats or proteins.

Glucose penetrates the cells and is used as energy to maintain vital functions of the body. In order for the organism to make use of glucose, it produces insulin which is a hormone that helps your body´s cells use the glucose.

Glycaemia is the glucose that circulates in the blood. The normal blood glucose level in human beings for non-diabetics should be stable between 75 to 110 mg/dL.

Basal Glycaemia is the amount of glucose present in the blood first thing in the morning after fasting (not eating for at least 8 hours).

Postprandial blood sugar measures blood glucose after eating a meal. The foods responsible for higher levels of glycaemia are those high in carbohydrates. In non-diabetic persons, although blood glucose levels rise after a meal, they return to normal approximately 2 hours later.

The following terminology can be differentiated:

    Insulin index: a measurement used to quantify the typical insulin response to various foods.
    Glycaemic Index: it is a number associated with a particular type of food that indicates the food´s effect on a person´s blood glucose (also called blood sugar) level.
    Glycaemic load: it is a number that estimates how much the food will raise a person´s blood glucose level after eating it.

A food can have a very high glycaemic index; nevertheless the amount consumed in a portion may not have a great incidence on glycaemia.

There are some clinical situations intermediate between normal levels and confirmed diabetes known as altered glucose metabolism, a term now used rather than pre-diabetes which has fallen into disuse. This alteration is:

    A metabolic alteration that is midway between normalcy and diabetes
    A risk factor for developing diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular illnesses

There are two clinical forms:

    Altered basal glycaemia: the stage used to define basal glycaemia between normal glycaemia and diabetes. It is defined between the 110-125 mg/dl margins, according to WHO and IDF.
    Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT): the stage defined by a plasma glyacemia in venous blood between 140 mg/dl and 200 mg/dl two hours after the 75 gr. glucose tolerance test.

It is considered Diabetes Mellitus if:

    The level of glycaemia in the blood plasma after fasting is equal to or greater than 126 mg/dl (7 mmol/l) in at least two tests.
    There are symptoms of diabetes and a random glycaemia level in the blood plasma greater than or equal to 200 mg/dl (11,1 mmol/l). It is not necessary to be fasting nor a second test.

There is a glycaemia level in the blood plasma equal to or greater than 200 mg/dl (11,1 mmol/l) two hours after the taking the Oral Glucose Tolerance Test with 75-gram glucose.
222.technical_description:
Described polymorphism rs560887 located in the G6PC2 gene is associated with fasting glucose plasma levels.

Several studies have demonstrated that healthy individuals with fasting plasma glucose levels (FPG) at the upper end of the normal range have higher risk of mortality. However, it was not associated with risk of developing Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. A study sought SNPs associated with fasting plasma glucose in 9,353 French and Finnish subjects who had lower blood glucose levels 6.1 mmol/L (110 mg/dL). The authors found that on average, each rs560887 (T) allele is linked to lower levels of fasting blood glucose.

475.name: Lung function (exhaled air volume)
475.description: Pulmonary function is an indicator of the physiological state of the airways and lungs, and plays an essential role in the diagnosis and monitoring of respiratory diseases, such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). When assessing lung function, spirometry is the most widely used test. This technique measures important parameters such as forced vital capacity (FVC).

232.name: Body mass index
232.description: Epidemiological studies indicate that one out of every three adults in the world is overweight. This abnormal accumulation of fat can be detrimental to health, although its consequences are not limited to the body, as it can influence mood and affect social life.
232.overview:
Overweight and obesity are defined as abnormal or excessive fat accumulation that may impair health.

Body mass index (BMI) is a simple index of weight-for-height that is commonly used to classify overweight and obesity in adults. It is defined as a person´s weight in kilograms divided by the square of his height in meters (kg/m2).

The WHO definition is:

    A BMI greater than or equal to 25 is overweight
    A BMI greater than or equal to 30 is obesity.

BMI provides the most useful population-level measure of overweight and obesity as it is the same for both sexes and for all ages of adults. However, it should be considered a rough guide because it may not correspond to the same degree of fatness in different individuals.
232.prevention:
Following a healthy lifestyle can help you prevent overweight and obesity. Many lifestyle habits begin during childhood. Thus, parents and families should encourage their children to make healthy choices, such as following a healthy diet and being physically active.

Make following a healthy lifestyle a family goal. For example:

    Follow a healthy eating plan. Make healthy food choices, keep your calorie needs and your family´s calorie needs in mind, and focus on the balance of energy IN and energy OUT.
    Focus on portion size. Watch the portion sizes in fast food and other restaurants. The portions served often are enough for two or three people. Children´s portion sizes should be smaller than those for adults. Cutting back on portion size will help you balance energy IN and energy OUT.
    Be active. Make personal and family time active. Find activities that everyone will enjoy. For example, go for a brisk walk, bike or rollerblade, or train together for a walk or run.
    Reduce screen time. Limit the use of TVs, computers, DVDs, and videogames because they limit time for physical activity. Health experts recommend 2 hours or less a day of screen time that´s not work- or homework-related.

Keep track of your weight, body mass index, and waist circumference. Also, keep track of your children´s growth.
232.technical_description:
Obesity is an important and very prevalent problem worldwide. People who are obese have a higher risk of developing hypertension, diabetes and other cardiovascular diseases.

As everyone knows, diet and exercise have a primary effect in reducing obesity, but it should be noted that genetics can influence the predisposition to be obese.

There are many polymorphisms related to obesity measures. Below are the SNPs polymorphisms analyzed in this technical report, as well as a brief summary of the scientific studies associated with these SNPs:

 -rs10871777:

The rs10871777 polymorphism, in addition to its relationship with obesity in adults, seems to be associated with obesity in children.

Individuals carrying the rs10871777-G allele are more likely to have a higher Body Mass Index (BMI).

-rs1121980 (c.46-34805G>A):

This polymorphism is located in the FTO gene ("fat mass and obesity associated gene") and was significantly associated with early onset obesity.

The allele associated with a greater predisposition to obesity is the A allele. Homozygous AA individuals have a higher risk of developing obesity, individuals carrying only one A allele (GA genotype) have a somewhat higher risk (but less than individuals with AA genotype), while individuals with GG genotype present a usual risk (not increased).

-rs12970134:

One of the studies related to this polymorphism indicates that the rs12970134-A allele is associated with obesity. The average increase in waist circumference is 0.9 cm BMI units per risk allele. In this sense, individuals with AA genotype have a higher risk of developing obesity than individuals with AG genotype and these have a higher risk than individuals with genotype GG (which present a typical risk)

-rs13130484:

One of the studies carried out in relation to this polymorphism was carried out in European individuals with a sample of 5261 obese individuals and 14106 controls. The TT genotype for this polymorphism is associated with 1.12 times more risk of obesity compared to individuals who present one copy of the C allele either homozygous (CC) or heterozygous (CT).

-rs1528133:

The rs1528133, rs2272382 and rs2272383 polymorphisms are located in the TUB gene.

In a study with 492 patients with type 2 diabetes, it was observed that rs1528133 polymorphism was associated with an increase in body mass index and therefore with the development of obesity. The allele associated with the development of obesity was the G allele.

In this same study two additional SNPs were described, rs2272382 and rs2272383, showing associations similar to these described for rs1528133.

A follow-up study of 1,680 middle-aged Dutch women found that the G allele of rs1528133 was significantly associated with weight gain and body mass index. In addition, carriers of the G allele were also associated with an increase in glycemic load compared to non-carriers.

-rs2229616:

The rs2229616 polymorphism (also known as V103I) located in the MC4R gene, has been associated with the regulation of appetite, body mass index, and therefore with obesity, as well as with other characteristics of the metabolic syndrome.

The rs2229616-T allele codes for the amino acid 103I (isoleucine), and this variant was associated, thanks to a study of more than 8,000 Caucasians, with a significant decrease in waist circumference.

In this study it was proposed that the rs2229616-T allele carriers are, in general, slightly thinner than the homozygous CC individuals for the rs2229616.

-rs2272382:

As previously said, the rs1528133, rs2272382 and rs2272383 polymorphisms are located in the TUB gene and have been associated with obesity risk.

For rs2272382, TT homozygous individuals and CT heterozygotes have been associated with a slightly higher body mass index than CC homozygotes.

-rs2272383:

The rs2272383 polymorphism is located in the TUB gene. In a study with 1680 middle-aged Dutch women, it was determined that the carriers of the G allele had an increase in body mass index.

-rs6971091:

This SNP or polymorphism is located on chromosome 7 and has so far been related to obesity. The A allele of this polymorphism is related to an increase in obesity

478.name: Diastolic blood pressure levels
478.description: Diastolic blood pressure is the pressure exerted between beats when the heart is at rest. The study of blood pressure levels is of utmost importance, especially when an increase in blood pressure is detected, as this constitutes a significant risk factor for the development of cardiovascular diseases.

556.name: Urate levels
556.description: Urate levels are an important marker of health that is influenced by a combination of dietary, metabolic, renal and genetic factors. Maintaining adequate urate levels is crucial to prevent painful and debilitating conditions such as gout and reduce the risk of cardiovascular and kidney disease.

552.name: Serum phosphate levels
552.description: Serum phosphate is a key mineral in the body that plays a crucial role in bone and tooth formation, energy generation and optimal nerve and muscle function. Alterations in blood phosphate levels may reflect various medical conditions and metabolic imbalances.

479.name: Intraocular pressure
479.description: Intraocular pressure (IOP) is the measure of the fluid pressure inside the eye and plays an essential role in maintaining the shape and function of the eyeball. Variations in IOP are linked to eye diseases such as glaucoma, retinal detachment and uveitis.

548.name: Calcium levels
548.description: Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the body, being essential in bone formation and maintenance, among many other important metabolic functions. It accounts for about 2% of body weight and accumulates mainly in the bones.

449.name: Vitamin C levels
449.description: Vitamin C is a water-soluble vitamin, an antioxidant and an essential cofactor for collagen biosynthesis, carnitine and catecholamine metabolism and dietary iron absorption.
449.overview:
Vitamin C or ascorbic acid is a water-soluble vitamin essential for optimal growth and development. It also plays an important role in tissue repair and in the formation of collagen, the most abundant protein in our body.

In addition, vitamin C plays an antioxidant role, helping to prevent damage caused by free radicals, which cause aging and can participate in diseases such as cancer, heart disease, or arthritis.

Water-soluble vitamins such as vitamin C dissolve in water, while the remaining amounts are excreted through urine.  We are not able to synthesize it and although our body keeps a small reserve, we must take it regularly to ensure that the supply is adequate.

Like most nutrients, our genes influence how the body absorbs, transports, and utilizes vitamin C. This can influence each individual. This can influence each person's risk of certain diseases differently and can make a difference in the minimum necessary amount of vitamin C that needs to be consumed each day.

The main functions of vitamin C are as follows:

Formation of collagen, the fundamental protein of skin, tendons, ligaments, and blood vessels.
Repair and maintenance of cartilage, bones, and teeth.
Participates in the healing of wounds.
Promotes the absorption of iron
Antioxidant function
449.technical_description:
The major transporters specific for vitamin C are encoded by a pair of genes called SLC23A1 and SLC23A2. Both are responsible for the maintenance of vitamin C concentrations in almost all cells (except erythrocytes), tissues, and extracellular fluids.

Vitamin C enters epithelial cells as ascorbic acid via sodium cotransporters or as dehydroascorbic acid (the oxidized form) via glucose transporters (GLUT1, GLUT3, and GLUT4) and the two sodium-dependent transporters mentioned above, SVCT1 or SLC23A1 and SVCT2 or SLC23A2.

In a very simplified form, the SVCT1 transporter is particularly important for absorption and protection against vitamin C losses in the kidney, while the SVCT2 transporter is important for intestinal absorption and transfer to tissues and the fetus. 

Knowledge of the different genetic variants in both transporters that affect vitamin C levels can also be used to determine the effect of vitamin C in different diseases.

Scurvy: Severe vitamin C deficiency can lead to a disease called scurvy, which causes anemia, bleeding gums, bruising and poor wound healing.

Cardiovascular disease: Scientific studies have found that vitamin C helps prevent heart attacks or strokes. Vitamin C has not been shown to lower cholesterol levels or reduce the overall risk of heart attacks, but it does help protect arteries from damage (atherosclerosis) by acting as an antioxidant. There are many studies that consider the concentration of vitamin C in the blood as a cardiovascular risk factor. 

Cancer: Role of vitamin C in cancer prevention, especially in those types that are not hormone-dependent but are diet-dependent, such as breast and colon cancer. 

Cataracts: Epidemiological studies show the inhibitory effect of vitamin C in the development of this disease.

Arthritis: Vitamin C is essential for the production of collagen, which is part of normal cartilage. Osteoarthritis destroys cartilage, putting pressure on bones and joints. Research suggests that free radicals may also be involved in cartilage destruction and that antioxidants, such as vitamin C, may limit these damaging effects. There is some evidence that people with a diet rich in vitamin C are less likely to suffer from osteoarthritis or rheumatoid arthritis.

SLC23A1 and SLC23A2 GENES 

The SLC23A1 gene encodes the SVCT1 (sodium-dependent vitamin C transporter 1) protein responsible for the absorption and reabsorption of vitamin C in the intestines and kidneys, respectively. The SLC23A2 gene encodes the SVCT2 (sodium-dependent vitamin C transporter 2) protein.

Studies suggest that SLC23A1 and SLC3A2 gene variants may negatively affect SVCT1 and SVCT2 protein function, respectively. The different variants of the SLC23A1 gene affect the ability of the SVCT1 protein to transport vitamin C and as a result, the reabsorption of vitamin C into the bloodstream by the kidneys may be impaired. This can lead to lower levels of vitamin C in the blood plasma. In turn, this reduces the supply of vitamin C to tissues such as the brain, skeletal muscle, and adrenal glands. 

Nutritional requirements and deficiency symptoms:

Males

9-13 years ----> 45 mg/day

14-18 years ----> 75 mg/day

19 >79 ----> 90 mg/day

Females: 

9-13 years ----> 45 mg/day

14-18 years ----> 65 mg/day

19 >79 ----> 75 mg/day

Pregnant women: 85 mg/day

Vitamin C intake should be increased in those situations in which there is a greater expenditure of this vitamin, such as in smokers or alcoholics, in people with intense physical activity (athletes) and, in short, in those physiological and pathological situations in which more vitamin C is required. The range of vitamin C concentration considered normal in plasma is very wide. Values range from 0.4 to 1.5 mg/dl, with low values being those between 0.2 and 0.4 mg/dl and deficiency values below 0.2 mg/dl. Plasma concentrations of vitamin C in men are lower than in women, and in both sexes, it decreases with age.

Signs of vitamin C deficiency include dry and brittle hair, inflammation of the gums, bleeding gums, rough, dry, and scaly skin, slow wound healing, easy bruising, nosebleeds, and a reduced ability to prevent infections. A severe form of vitamin C deficiency is scurvy.

Vitamin C deficiency can be prevented by adequate vitamin C intake. Vitamin C is present in fruits and vegetables, in greater or lesser amounts, being scarce in cereals. The richest fruits are the acidic ones, since the low pH stabilizes vitamin C, as for example kiwi, strawberry, currants, mango, and orange. In foods of animal origin, it is not very abundant, and above all, it is present in liver, kidney, and brain.

545.name: Apolipoprotein B levels
545.description: Apolipoprotein B or ApoB is a key protein in maintaining the structure of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) or "bad cholesterol" particles and other larger lipid particles that carry cholesterol esters and triglycerides in varying amounts and proportions through the bloodstream.

566.name: Body fat percentage
566.description: Body fat percentage is an essential indicator of health that is influenced by a combination of modifiable factors, such as diet and exercise, and non-modifiable factors, such as age, sex, and genetics. Understanding these factors and their interaction can aid in the development of personalized strategies for weight management and health promotion.

228.name: Lactose intolerance
228.description: Lactose is the main carbohydrate present in milk. Mammalian offspring are able to digest it thanks to lactase into simpler sugars (glucose and galactose) that are easily absorbed in the intestine. There are certain genetic variants that could explain why some people find it easier to digest lactose in adulthood while others end up developing intolerance.
228.overview: Lactose intolerance becomes evident when an organism has a pathologic response to the sugars found in milk and cannot digest the glucose (lactose).
228.symptoms: The signs and symptoms of lactose intolerance usually begin 30 minutes to two hours after drinking milk or having dairy products (yogurt, cheese, cream, etc.) or other products containing lactose (pastries, ice cream, chocolate).  Some of the most characteristic symptoms of lactose intolerance are: stomachache, nausea, diarrhea and gas and its effects vary from person to person in intensity.
228.prevention: In only one of the three types of lactose intolerance can this disorder be prevented. This can be done by preventing the mentioned diseases, some of which are also congenital. Patients can improve over time with the proper treatment and diet.
228.technical_description:
The rs182549 polymorphism is a polymorphism that has been associated with lactose intolerance and is located in the MCM6 gene. Genetic variations in MCM6 are related to lactose intolerance, since the MCM6 gene is a gene that acts over the lactase gene (LCT), which is the gene responsible for lactose digestion. In European Caucasian populations, the rs182549-C allele, which is the most common allele, is the one that confers a risk for lactose intolerance in Caucasian individuals.

Homozygous individuals for the C allele (CC genotype) are likely to be lactose intolerant, as a result of the physiological decrease in lactase activity in intestinal cells.

The rs4988235 polymorphism is also located in the MCM6 gene and influences the LCT lactase gene.

In European Caucasian populations, the rs4988235-A allele is the most common allele and is associated with the persistence of lactase production in adult life and therefore a greater possibility of lactose digestion and tolerance.

Homozygous individuals for the G allele of this polymorphism (GG genotype) are likely to be lactose intolerant, as a result of the physiological decrease in lactase activity in intestinal cells. But occasionally, GG genotype individuals can be lactose tolerant.

This is because there are other environmental and genetic factors that affect the digestion of lactose.

382.name: Tendinopathies in lower extremities (legs)
382.description: The probability of suffering tendinopathy in the lower extremities, in addition to being affected by a multitude of environmental factors such as exercise, is also affected by the genetic component. Variants in the genes that regulate the development and maintenance of bones, cartilage and tendons play a fundamental role in the vulnerability of certain individuals to injury.
382.overview:
Tendons are anatomical structures located between the muscle and the bone, whose function is to transmit the force generated by the muscle to the bone, giving rise to the joint movement.

Tendons are composed mostly of collagen (30%) and water (68%), with a small proportion of elastin (2%). On the other hand, the vascularization of the same is limited, but increases during the exercise and before the healing processes.

The term “tendinitis” itself refers to an inflammatory situation, therefore, currently tend to evolve with the concept of tendinopathies as a general and global entity (in which not always histological studies have shown inflammatory processes with markers of inflammation in the associated injury).

Currently and following the guidelines of the International Rheumatology Association, tendinopathies are classified according to the inflammation process:

Inflammatory tendinopathies: Paratendinitis (inflammation of the paratendon, synovial covered or not, where the component of the edema predominates, due to an insult between the tendon and the sliding tissue. The mainly associated clinic is crepitus, pain, local sensitivity and dysfunction) and Tendinitis (tendon degeneration with tendinous and vascular rupture associated with an inflammatory response to repair. It usually occurs with sudden onset of pain, bruising, external inflammatory symptoms and loss of strength and dysfunction).
Noninflammatory tendinopathies: Tendinosis (intratendinous degenerative process without signs of inflammatory response with atrophy, mainly due to aging, microtrauma or vascular compromise, which usually presents with pain, loss of strength, dysfunction and painful tendon nodules) and Injuries due to repetitive efforts or overload.

One of the main characteristics associated with the tendon is its modulation capacity, and therefore its potential ability to return to its basal state included after damage. For this it is important to know the intrinsic, individual, genetic, biomechanical and environmental factors that interact with each other and that prevent and optimize the recovery of the tendinopathies.

The influence of genetics on tendon pathologies is increasingly known, which allows a more exhaustive knowledge of the interpretation of genetic variation, not only in the etiopathogenesis but also in other aspects such as susceptibility, prognosis and the individual response to treatments.

ACHILLES TENDINOPATHY

Achilles tendon is the largest and strongest of the body, connecting the sural triceps  with the heel, performing plantar flexion of the foot and participating in the knee flexion. Holds a great tension, and therefore, presents a high risk of injury when doing any movement (running, jumping, speed changes, etc.).

The associated risk factors are usually an excessive use, repetition traumatisms, vascular diseases, genetic predisposition, neuropathies and rheumatological diseases that can cause degeneration of the tendon.

INJURY OF QUADRICEPS TENDONS

Femoral quadriceps is a large, powerful muscle found in the anterior part of the thigh. The muscles and tendons that comprise it form contractile units that stabilize the hip and knee and allow their movement.

Injuries of the muscles and tendons of the quadriceps are common among athletes and active adults. The majority of these injuries can be diagnosed through a careful history and physical examination, and conservative management is successful for the vast majority, including rest, physiotherapy and analgesia.

BREAKAGE OF CRUCIATE LIGAMENTS

The stability of the knee is ensured by four ligaments: the anterior and posterior cruciate ligaments and the lateral and internal lateral ligaments.

The cruciate ligaments are two structures that cross the inside of the knee, join the tibia with the femur and provide stability in the movements of extension and flexion.

There are two types of cruciate ligaments:

Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL, prevents the tibia from moving forward with respect to the femur. Its breakage is the most frequent) and the Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL, its function is to prevent the tibia from moving backwards).

The injury of these ligaments occurs after a sudden change of direction of the knee, an exaggerated deceleration or a contusion. It is usually unilateral and the rupture may be complete or partial, and may be combined with the fracture of other related structures (collateral ligaments, posterior cruciate ligament or meniscus).
382.technical_description:
Type I collagen is the major protein that makes up tendons and ligaments. Type I collagen is a heterotrimer composed of chains or fibrils encoded by the COL1A1 and COL1A2 genes. Mutations in the COL1A1 gene can cause connective tissue diseases such as osteogenesis imperfecta or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, as well as other less dramatic tendon and ligament disorders that may be related to athletic performance.

Polymorphisms within the COL1A1 gene are associated with deleterious or conversely advantageous effects with respect to soft musculoskeletal tissue injuries, such as cruciate ligament ruptures, shoulder dislocation, Achilles tendon ruptures (RUP) and Achilles tendinopathies (AT or TEN). The genetic polymorphism c.104-441 C>A in the COL1A1 gene (codenamed rs1800012 in clinical databases) involving a C nucleotide change to an A nucleotide causes an increase in COL1A1 protein levels and may lead to a more robust synthesis of type I collagen. Three studies in different populations have shown that the AA genotype, which is found at low frequency in the general population, has a protective role against soft tissue injuries, in particular, this protection has been seen against cruciate ligament ruptures, Achilles tendon ruptures, other Achilles tendinopathies and shoulder dislocations.

The TIMP2 gene codes for the production of an inhibitor of certain proteases called metallopeptidases that degrade the extracellular matrix of soft tissues. There are two genetic studies that demonstrate the association of a genetic polymorphism in this gene associated with Achilles tendon pathology (ATP). This is the g.78928193A>G polymorphism (defined with the code rs4789932 in clinical databases). The two studies were conducted by Khoury et al. with different populations and gave different results. The 2013 study was conducted in 421 South African and Australian Caucasian subjects (173 with tendinopathy and 248 controls), and the AG genotype was observed to be over-represented in those individuals at risk of Achilles tendinopathies (often abbreviated as AT or TEN) and was observed equally in males and females. However, in the 2016 study carried out by the same team but with 249 British Caucasian individuals (118 with tendinopathy and 131 with controls), they observed that in this population the GG genotype was the one that was overrepresented not only in Achilles tendinopathies but also in those individuals with tendon rupture (RUP). Furthermore, this genotype associated with risk of Achilles tendinopathies and ruptures was this time more associated with males than females. What does seem to be clear is that there is a direct link between the AA genotype and protection against Achilles tendon pathologies.

Collagen fibril, which consists predominantly of type I collagen, is the main structural component of tendons, ligaments, and other non-cartilaginous connective tissues. The formation and diameter of the collagen fibril is regulated, among other molecules, by the minor fibrillar type V collagen protein. The V-type collagen isoform comprises two α1 (V) and one α2 (V) chain, encoded by the COL5A1 and COL5A2 genes, respectively, and forms between 1% and 5% of the total collagen content. The COL5A1 gene is the most explored genetic locus in relation to tendon and ligament injuries, while mutations in the COL5A2 gene have been identified in Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, a disease characterised by joint hypermobility, muscle laxity and hypotonia.

There is a widely studied polymorphism in the COL5A1 gene associated with tendinopathies and ligament ruptures. This is the rs12722 C/T polymorphism (c*267C>T) located in the 3' region of the gene that is not translated but affects its expression. This single nucleotide polymorphism within the COL5A1 gene (SNP; rs12722 C/T has been previously associated with tendon and ligament pathologies. Given the high incidence of tendon and ligament injuries in elite athletes, the hypothesis that this SNP would be associated with career success is widely hypothesised. Heffernan S. M. et al, conducted a study in 2016 with 1105 participants (565 controls and 548 elite rugby players) where they demonstrated that both the CT and CC genotypes were overrepresented in elite rugby athletes compared to control individuals. In this study the authors propose that the presence of one or two C alleles is beneficial for athletes and provides heritable resistance to soft tissue injuries such as tendon and ligament injuries in the lower extremities. The CT genotype confers resistance, and the CC genotype confers even greater resistance to such injuries.

A 2016 study by Kirk et al. showed that this polymorphism was also implicated, not only in the risk of Achilles tendinopathies and ligament injuries in the lower leg, but also in the upper leg. Specifically, they showed that the C allele of the rs12722 SNP has a protective effect on the mechanical stiffness of the tendons of the quadriceps muscles. Athletes carrying the C variant suffer less contractures. This genetic association with the risk of quadriceps tendon injuries is also corroborated by two further studies:

Galasso et al and Longo et al also saw the association of the protective effect of the C allele against bilateral spontaneous ruptures of the quadriceps tendon, where those individuals with TT genotypes were significantly more predisposed to suffer these ruptures.
Another study carried out by Stępień-Słodkowska et al in 2015 in skiers demonstrated the same association of the rs12722 C/T SNP in skiers (specifically in female skiers), those with the C variant had a lower risk of suffering anterior cruciate ligament injuries.

With regard to Achilles tendinopathies, the protective effect of the C allele versus the T allele in this SNP of the COL5A1 gene has also been demonstrated, as described by Mokone et al in 2005 in a South African Caucasian and Australian population (111 patients and 129 controls), and by September et al in 2009 (178 patients and 342 controls), who demonstrated that those individuals with the CC genotype had a lower risk of suffering Achilles tendinopathies.

What remains to be clarified with respect to Achilles Tendon Pathologies (ATP) is whether it is necessary to have both C alleles of the rs12722 C/T SNP or whether the presence of just one C allele is sufficient. Different authors and clinicians disagree. If we take as an example the other tendinopathies that we have cited associated with this SNP, we could say that one copy of the C allele confers some protection while two copies of the C allele confer even greater protection.  The presence of two T alleles (TT genotype) always confers an increased risk of tendon and ligament injuries in both upper and lower leg regions and in all types of athletes.

These data have very relevant implications for the management of inter-individual differences in injury risk among elite athletes, type of training, type of special care, etc.

In addition to the importance of this rs12722 C/T polymorphism in the COL5A1 gene for multiple tendinopathies independently, there are studies by Raleigh et al. that show that the CC genotype of this rs if combined with an AA or AG genotype in the rs679620 SNP of the MMP3 gene (see MMP3 gene tables) has an even more protective effect on Achilles tendinopathies. The lower risk of suffering Achilles tendinopathy (AT or TEN) occurs when these two events occur together.

The protein encoded by the GDF-5 gene (also known as bone morphogenic protein-14 or BMP-14), is involved in the maintenance, development and repair of bone, cartilage and other soft musculoskeletal tissues, such as tendons and ligaments.

In 2010 Posthumus et al. found an association between individuals with Achilles Tendon Pathology (ATP) and the SNP c.-275G>A of the GDF-5 gene (codenamed rs143383 in clinical databases). The study, which included 132 patients with Achilles tendinopathy (AT or TEN), 39 patients with Achilles tendon rupture (RUP) and 238 individuals without tendinopathy, showed that the AA genotype was found at a high frequency in patients with tendinopathies and Achilles tendon ruptures.

ADAMTS family proteins are metalloproteases that, among other functions, are involved in the degradation of proteoglycans and pro-collagen fibrils of the extracellular matrix of tissues. Abnormalities in the expression of these metalloproteases can lead to deleterious or beneficial effects in tendons.

The presence of the c.3155 A>G variant in the ADAMTS14 gene (also coded as rs4747096 in clinical databases) is associated with a delayed age of onset of Achilles tendinopathies. A study carried out by El Khoury et al. in 2013 showed that carrying a G allele already confers a delay in the development of Achilles tendinopathies, while if the genotype for this SNP is GG, the age of onset of this type of lesion is delayed even further (from 39 years for the AA genotype to 55 years for the GG genotype).

The MMP3 gene is part of a family of genes called MMPs (matrix metalloproteases) involved in the breakdown of the extracellular matrix in normal physiological processes, such as tissue remodelling. MMP3 codes for a protease that degrades fibronectin, laminin, collagen types III, IV, IX and X and proteoglycans (components of tendons and ligaments). Genetic polymorphisms in MMP3 may therefore affect the breakdown of soft tissues such as tendons and ligaments. In fact, it has been described that the c.133 A>G polymorphism (or c.133 T>C, its complementary strand and the direction in which we genotype this item) in this gene (named as rs679620 in clinical databases) is associated with Achilles tendinopathies, in particular the CC genotype for this polymorphism is overrepresented in Achilles tendon ruptures (RUP). This association has been verified by two studies, both involving Caucasian individuals. One study was conducted by Khoury et al. in 2016 with 249 British Caucasian individuals (118 with tendinopathy and 131controls) and the other study was conducted by Raleigh et al. in 2009 with 212 South African Caucasian individuals (114 had Achilles tendinopathy symptoms and 98 control individuals).

In addition to the importance of this rs679620 c.133 A>G (or c.133 T>C, its complementary strand) polymorphism in the MMP3 gene, there are studies showing that the TT or TC genotype of this rs if combined with a CC genotype in the rs12722 SNP of the COL5A1 gene (see COL5A1 gene tables) causes an even more protective effect in Achilles tendinopathies. The lower risk of suffering from Achilles tendinopathy (AT or TEN) occurs when these two events occur together

The TNC gene codes for the tenascin-C glycoprotein. This protein is particularly highly expressed in the extracellular matrix of musculoskeletal tissues that are exposed to tensile and compressive stress during the transmission of mechanical forces, such as tendons and ligaments. Tenascin-C is involved in cell-extracellular matrix adhesion and therefore genetic polymorphisms that may vary the function of the protein may affect the risk of tendinopathies.

Saunders et al. in 2013 found that the rs2104772 polymorphism consisting of an A nucleotide change to a T nucleotide at position c.5029A>T had an association with Achilles tendinopathies (AT or TEN) in South African Caucasian and Australian individuals, with the A allele being protective against tendinopathies, while the T allele is associated with an increased predisposition to Achilles tendinopathy. The substitution of the A nucleotide by a T nucleotide produces an amino acid change in the TNC protein consisting of the substitution of an isoleucine at position 1677 by a leucine. According to these studies, individuals with one copy of the polymorphism (TA genotype for that rs) will have a certain risk of suffering Achilles tendinopathies, while individuals with two copies of the polymorphism (TT genotype for that rs) will have a higher risk of suffering Achilles tendinopathies.

383.name: Tendinopathies in upper extremities (arms)
383.description: Tendons are anatomical structures located between the muscle and the bone, whose function is to transmit the force generated by the muscle to the bone, giving rise to joint movement. The influence of genetics in tendon pathologies is increasingly known, which allows a more exhaustive knowledge of the interpretation of genetic variations, not only in the causes of tendinopathies but also in other aspects such as susceptibility, prognosis and individual response to treatments.
383.overview:
Tendons are anatomical structures located between the muscle and the bone, whose function is to transmit the force generated by the muscle to the bone, giving rise to the joint movement.

Tendons are composed mostly of collagen (30%) and water (68%), with a small proportion of elastin (2%). On the other hand, the vascularization of the same is limited, but increases during the exercise and before the healing processes.

The term “tendinitis” itself refers to an inflammatory situation, therefore, currently tend to evolve with the concept of tendinopathies as a general and global entity (in which not always histological studies have shown inflammatory processes with markers of inflammation in the associated injury).

One of the main characteristics associated with the tendon is its modulation capacity, and therefore its potential ability to return to its basal state included after damage. For this it is important to know the intrinsic, individual, genetic, biomechanical and environmental factors that interact with each other and that prevent and optimize the recovery of the tendinopathies.

The influence of genetics on tendon pathologies is increasingly known, which allows a more exhaustive knowledge of the interpretation of genetic variation, not only in the etiopathogenesis but also in other aspects such as susceptibility, prognosis and the individual response to treatments.

INJURIES IN THE ROTATOR CUFF

The rotator cuff muscles are small, stabilizing muscles of the shoulder joint, which allow the movements of the shoulder to be precise and coordinated. It is composed of four muscles (supraspinatus, subscapularis, minor round and infraspinatus) and their responsible tendons.

Of the four muscles of the rotator cuff, the tendon that is most frequently injured is the supraspinatus. The lesion of the same occurs mainly due to the degeneration of the tissues caused by a deficit in the vascularization, by a direct trauma or by an overload suffered due to an exhaustive use for different workloads.
383.technical_description:
Type I collagen is the major protein that makes up tendons and ligaments. Type I collagen is a heterotrimer composed of chains or fibrils encoded by the COL1A1 and COL1A2 genes. Mutations in the COL1A1 gene can cause connective tissue diseases such as osteogenesis imperfecta or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, as well as other less dramatic tendon and ligament disorders that may be related to athletic performance.

Polymorphisms within the COL1A1 gene are associated with deleterious or conversely advantageous effects with respect to soft musculoskeletal tissue injuries, such as cruciate ligament ruptures, shoulder dislocation, Achilles’ tendon ruptures (RUP) and Achilles tendinopathies (AT or TEN). The genetic polymorphism c.104-441 C>A in the COL1A1 gene (codenamed rs1800012 in clinical databases) involving a C nucleotide change to an A nucleotide causes an increase in COL1A1 protein levels and may lead to a more robust synthesis of type I collagen.

Three studies in different populations have shown that the AA genotype, which is found in low frequency in the general population, has a protective role against soft tissue injuries, in particular, this protection has been seen against cruciate ligament ruptures, Achilles’ tendon ruptures, other Achilles tendinopathies and shoulder dislocations.

The DEFB1 or β-defensin 1 gene has been associated with muscle degeneration in mice. Several diseases are related to dysregulation of this gene, such as muscular dystrophy and cystic fibrosis.

There is evidence showing genetic factors as an intrinsic risk factor in athletes for susceptibility to injury of the rotator cuff, which is the capsule formed by a fusion of tendons that anchors the arm to the shoulder and which is frequently injured in athletes.

Recently, studies have been carried out in which polymorphisms in this gene have been related to degenerative processes in the tendons that form part of the rotator cuff (RCD, from the English "Rotatory Cuff Diseases"). A trial conducted by Motta et al. in 2014 with 410 individuals (203 with cuff tendon lesions and 207 controls) showed that the c.-44G>C polymorphism (codenamed rs1800972 in clinical databases) is associated with RCD. In particular, the authors describe that the G allele offers a protective effect against this type of lesions, the GG genotype was much less represented in lesioned individuals than in controls, as was the GC genotype. Individuals with the CC genotype are at increased risk of RCD.

In addition to association with Achilles tendinopathies, polymorphisms have also been found in the tenascin-C gene (TNC) that are associated with risk of injuries to the tendons that attach the arm to the shoulder and that occur frequently in athletes, RTC (Rotator Cuff Tears). According to a 2017 study by Kluger R. et al. with 156 patients and 76 controls, the rs1138545 polymorphism (c.3497 C>T) is associated with RTC patients, with these individuals showing a high rate of the CC genotype compared to controls, demonstrating that the T allele in this polymorphism confers a protective effect and thus a lower risk of RTC lesions. Patients homozygous for the C allele (i.e. possessing two copies of the C allele, CC genotype) had more recurrence and more failure to heal lesions, as well as more severe recurrent events. In addition, it is important to note that the protective effect of the T allele is more significant in ruptures that may be more severe than in less severe ruptures. The molecular explanation for this protective effect remains to be elucidated, while it is known that this change of the C nucleotide to a T nucleotide produces a change in the protein from an arginine amino acid to a histidine amino acid in a region of the protein that connects two important domains of the protein and is involved in its contacts with the extracellular matrix of soft tissues. This change could favour tendon grip and could explain the protective effect of this gene variant.

384.name: Exercise-induced muscle damage (initial phase)
384.description: The presence of certain genetic variants could explain why some people are less likely to be injured than others, as well as having an easier time recovering from prolonged and strenuous exercise. Knowing how someone will respond to a particular type of exercise can guide us in personalizing our workouts, while reducing the risk of injury associated with muscle overload.
384.overview:
Prolonged unaccustomed exercise involving muscle lengthening (eccentric) actions can result in ultrastructural muscle disruption, impaired excitation–contraction coupling, inflammation and muscle protein degradation.

Certain gene variations, or polymorphisms have been associated with exercise-induced muscle damage (i.e. individuals with certain genotypes experience greater muscle damage, and require longer recovery, following strenuous exercise). Knowing how someone is likely to respond to a particular type of exercise could help coaches/practitioners individualise the exercise training of their athletes/patients, thus maximising recovery and adaptation, while reducing overload-associated injury risk.

PHASES OF MUSCLE DAMAGE

The exercise can produce muscle damage through a series of alterations of two types, some of early onset and mechanical origin while others are consequences of the previous ones and consist on the inflammatory process. The most used models are the eccentric exercise (by isolated contractions, jumps or downhill running) and the post-competition analysis of some test, for example marathon.

This type of exercise called eccentric exercise (rapid stretching of the muscle by contraction) is successfully included in the training of different sport contexts improving strength, muscular power, coordination and performance through the improvement in the recruitment of motor units, a increase in reflex enhancement and changes in the elastic properties of muscle and connective tissue. However, by containing an eccentric exercise phase, it is highly associated with muscle damage and pain.

As older people appear to be more susceptible to exercise-induced muscle damage than younger adults, older people with a genetic predisposition to greater muscle damage, may be at a greater risk of developing muscle–tendon unit injury.

THE INITIAL PHASE OF EXERCISE-INDUCED MUSCLE DAMAGE involves damage to the muscular structures responsible for the contraction of the myofibrils and tends to cause damage to the proteins that form these mechanical structures, such as collagen, actin, myosin, desmin, titin and other proteins that interact with the cellular cytoskeleton.
384.technical_description:
Exercise can produce muscle damage through a series of alterations of two types, some of which are of early onset and of mechanical origin while others are consequences of the former and consist of the inflammatory process.

The first polymorphism we have analysed (shown in the table above) refers to the initial phase of muscle damage. Immediately after exercise, particularly in exercises with eccentric contractions (the muscle develops tension by lengthening, i.e., extending its length) especially in those individuals who are not used to it, there is evidence of injury to the skeletal muscle fibres: a) alteration of the normal structures of the muscle myofibrils, observable with light and optical microscopy and b) loss of intramuscular proteins (e.g. creatine kinase enzymes). b) loss of intramuscular proteins (e.g., creatine kinase enzymes, CK) in plasma, indicating damage to muscle structures involved in muscle contraction. This pathology is probably responsible for the temporary reductions in muscle strength and delayed onset soreness that can occur after eccentric exercise. Example: when lowering weights, the quadriceps muscle is extended, and an eccentric contraction is occurring. Here the insertion points of the quadriceps muscle move away from each other, hence we say that an eccentric contraction is occurring.

The type of exercise referred to as eccentric exercise is successfully included in training in a variety of sporting contexts to improve strength, muscle power, coordination and performance through improved motor unit recruitment, increased reflex potentiation and changes in the elastic properties of the muscle and connective tissue. However, as it contains an eccentric exercise phase, it is highly associated with muscle damage and soreness.

There is evidence that the initial muscle damage produced by this type of exercise may be associated with various genetic polymorphisms or SNPs in genes encoding for muscle fibre structural proteins, for extracellular matrix proteins or for proteins that bind fibres to the extracellular matrix.
