An example of these polymorphisms associated with muscle damage is found in the α3 Actinin gene (ACTN) and is c.1729C>T (p.Arg577Ter) or rs1815739 (code with which it is named in clinical databases) and which consists of a substitution of a C nucleotide for a T nucleotide, and which in the amino acid sequence of the protein involves a change of an arginine amino acid for a stop codon, resulting in a shorter and therefore less functional protein. This SNP is also known by the nomenclature ACTN3 R577X, where R is the arginine and X stands for the end of the protein (X is a stop codon of protein synthesis). The proportion of homozygous XX individuals in the general population varies from less than 1% in Africans, 18% in Europeans and 25% in Asians.

The ACTN3 gene codes for a muscle-tendon interaction protein, and this R577X polymorphism has implications for the ability to recover after strenuous exercise for the muscle and thus influences the risk of muscle injury (as shown in studies by Pimenta et al. 20; Vincent et al. 2010; Deuster et al. 2013). This is particularly important in elite athletes, who are known to possess a different genetic profile than the general population. An individual with a TT (or also called XX) genotype is not able to produce the α3-actinin protein, hence the difficulty in recovery. The absence of α3-actinin, however, does not result in disease due to the compensatory effect of α2-actinin, but it does affect fitness.

The ACTN3 XX genotype is associated with lower muscle volume, poorer sprint times, lower strength and lower muscle power. These individuals are approximately 3 times more susceptible to strenuous exercise-induced rhabdomyolysis (breakdown of muscle tissue that releases the contents of muscle fibres into the blood) than RR or RX individuals. However, care must be taken with the interpretation of these genetic results as there are other studies that show no differences between the 3 different genotypes that can occur in this SNP, and therefore, genotyping must be contextualised with the physiology and problems of the athlete.

In addition to the implications that ACTN3 has on eccentric or power exercise muscle injuries, ACTN3 deficiency is also a factor in classifying a potential athlete as an endurance or power athlete. Studies have been conducted on muscle fibres and ACTN3 genotype. A study of a group of elite Australian athletes found that at least in females, the R allele was associated with sprinters, while the X allele was associated with endurance athletes. Such an association was also found in male athletes but with a weaker tendency. Endurance athletes had an overrepresented XX genotype compared to power athletes. This indicates that endurance athletes may benefit from the absence of α3-actinin. This fact can be explained if it is understood that muscle damage induced by eccentric actions could lead to increased degradation of proteins connecting to the extracellular matrix and adjacent muscle myofibrils, and this would explain the increased susceptibility to muscle injury in XX homozygotes performing power exercise and no involvement or even a beneficial involvement in those endurance athletes, since alpha-actinin-3 protein is only used in fast twitch muscle fibres (more exploited by power athletes). In addition, alpha-actinin-3 is synthesised only in type II skeletal muscle fibres, which are the fast twitch fibres, and are more susceptible to muscle damage from eccentric or power exercise or contractions.

Another gene whose polymorphisms have been associated with early stage muscle damage (mechanical damage affecting structural components of myofibres) is the myosin 2 gene and more specifically the SNP is in the myosin light-chain kinase unit (MYLK2). Myosin light-chain kinase increases Ca2+ sensitivity by phosphorylating other proteins involved in muscle contraction, thereby increasing force in muscle fibres. Like actinin-alpha-3, it is expressed in type II muscle fibres (the fast twitch fibres associated with eccentric exercise). The SNP c.62G>T (p.Pro21His) also referred to as rs28497577 in clinical databases is implicated in muscle strength loss and increased blood levels of CK (creatine kinase, a blood marker of muscle breakdown) following strenuous exercise according to a study by Deuster et al. in 2013. The authors observed that individuals carrying a T allele (both GT and TT genotypes) were at much higher risk of strenuous exercise-induced rhabdomyolysis compared to individuals with a GG genotype. This would explain why African-American athletes are at high risk of rhabdomyolysis, as the allele frequency of the T allele is much higher than in other population groups and they also have a higher proportion of type II muscle fibres compared to Caucasian individuals.

This study coincides with another carried out by Clarkson et al. in 2005, which showed an exaggerated concentration of plasma CK (a marker of muscle damage) in individuals carrying the T allele.

385.name: Exercise-induced muscle damage (second phase)
385.description: In the second phase of exercise-induced muscle damage, an inflammatory process takes place in which certain genetic variants may contribute to more or less muscle damage.
385.overview:
Certain gene variations, or polymorphisms have been associated with exercise-induced muscle damage (i.e. individuals with certain genotypes experience greater muscle damage, and require longer recovery, following strenuous exercise). Knowing how someone is likely to respond to a particular type of exercise could help coaches/practitioners individualise the exercise training of their athletes/patients, thus maximising recovery and adaptation, while reducing overload-associated injury risk.

PHASES OF MUSCLE DAMAGE

The exercise can produce muscle damage through a series of alterations of two types, some of early onset and mechanical origin while others are consequences of the previous ones and consist on the inflammatory process.

THE SECOND PHASE OF EXERCISE-INDUCED MUSCLE DAMAGE consists of an inflammatory response. The degenerative products of the muscle fiber, especially the tissue polysaccharides, through the process called chemotaxis, produce that the leukocytes approach the cell to start the inflammatory process. With muscle damage induced by exercise, there is also an increase in cytokines, mainly IL1B, IL6 and TNFα, activated by the increase in free radicals and prostaglandins, which increase the inflammatory process. Polymorphisms or variants in the genes that code for these cytokines seem to be involved in the development of this second inflammatory phase of muscle damage
385.technical_description:
The cytokine TNF ("tumour necrosis factor") is a pro-inflammatory factor whose circulation in plasma is associated with metabolic disorders. TNF induces insulin resistance in skeletal muscle. TNF levels are slightly increased in plasma after intense exercise, however, locally, in muscle fibres, TNF increases significantly after strenuous exercise. This is of relevance as TNF activates the catabolic pathway of muscle protein degradation, and high plasma TNF levels are associated with reduced fitness.

The -308 G>A polymorphism, which is located in the area that regulates TNF synthesis (codenamed rs1800629 in clinical databases), is associated with increased plasma TNF levels, as described by Karimi et al. in 2009. The presence of a single A variant already leads to increased levels of this interleukin. The presence of this A allele in this SNP is associated with reduced fitness in women was also demonstrated by Pereira et al. in 2013.

The second phase of muscle damage is a complex event linked to inflammatory processes. Leukocytes infiltrate the damaged muscle fibres and remain there for days or even weeks. Neutrophils degrade the damaged muscle tissue by producing oxidising agents which in turn attract macrophages to the trauma site. The macrophages eventually promote the activation of muscle satellite cells that are responsible for regenerating the tissue.

This mechanism of muscle damage and repair is orchestrated by various cytokines that modulate the inflammatory response. Neutrophils and macrophages that have migrated to the injury express the cytokine TNF (tumour necrosis factor). TNF activates the protein degradation pathway via the proteasome, which is why this factor is important in the processes of injury and remodelling of muscle tissue and polymorphisms in this gene have been associated with the second stage of exercise-induced muscle damage. Other genes in which polymorphisms associated with this second stage of muscle damage have been described include other cytokine genes such as IL1B and IL6.

Approximately 5 days after eccentric or power exercise, there is an increase in the levels of the cytokine IL1B which, at a systemic level is marginal, but at a local level in the muscle fibres the increase is much higher, producing tissue inflammation. IL1B acts synergistically with TNF (tumour necrosis factor) and induces the expression of other pro-inflammatory genes. Denis et al. in 2004 investigated the association of polymorphisms in the IL1B gene with inflammatory response in 24 Caucasian men after endurance exercise. Participants with the c.315 G>A polymorphism (also referred to as rs1143634 in clinical databases), which consists of a G nucleotide change to an A nucleotide at that position in the gene, were protected against inflammation. Individuals with the GG genotype had an increased inflammatory response after exercise, while those individuals with at least one copy of the A allele were protected against exacerbated inflammation.

Following eccentric exercise, muscle can induce local expression of the cytokine IL-6 with pro-inflammatory properties. In these cases the systemic expression of IL-6 is lower than in other types of sport such as running, but the local expression is higher.

A functional SNP has been described in the region that regulates IL6 synthesis, polymorphism -174 G>C (codenamed rs1800795 in clinical databases) in which the presence of two C alleles (CC genotype) have a higher plasma CK concentration (creatine kinase or CK is a marker in the blood that reveals muscle damage) after strenuous muscle exercise than individuals with GG or GC genotype, as described by Yamin et al. in their 2008 and 2009 studies.

Although these indications have yet to be confirmed, there are studies such as the one by Ruiz JR in 2010, which showed that GG or GC genotypes are more frequent in power athletes than in endurance athletes or in the normal population, as muscle inflammation due to power exercise and the associated damage is lower. Although both the GG and GC genotypes confer protection against muscle damage, the GG genotype confers a greater benefit to its carriers as it provides even greater recovery and increased proliferation of satellite cells (the cells that repair injured muscle tissues).

However, one might think that this relationship between allelic variants and local muscle damage and inflammation is contradictory, as the G allele leads to increased local secretion of IL-6 and therefore increased local inflammation. However, this local increase in IL-6 after eccentric or strenuous exercise activates satellite cells responsible for muscle regeneration and stiffening. Thus, although it would initially appear that a local excess of IL-6 could be detrimental, this is not the case in that it facilitates local muscle regeneration (except in athletes with chronic low-grade systemic inflammation, where the presence of a G allele could be detrimental).

386.name: Exercise-induced muscle damage (regeneration capacity)
386.description: Variants affecting genes involved in the activation of the cells responsible for muscle regeneration are relevant to understanding why some people require more time to recover after a muscle injury.
386.overview:
Prolonged unaccustomed exercise involving muscle lengthening (eccentric) actions can result in ultrastructural muscle disruption, impaired excitation–contraction coupling, inflammation and muscle protein degradation.

Certain gene variations, or polymorphisms have been associated with exercise-induced muscle damage (i.e. individuals with certain genotypes experience greater muscle damage, and require longer recovery, following strenuous exercise). Knowing how someone is likely to respond to a particular type of exercise could help coaches/practitioners individualise the exercise training of their athletes/patients, thus maximising recovery and adaptation, while reducing overload-associated injury risk.

PHASES OF MUSCLE DAMAGE

The exercise can produce muscle damage through a series of alterations of two types, some of early onset and mechanical origin while others are consequences of the previous ones and consist on the inflammatory process. The most used models are the eccentric exercise (by isolated contractions, jumps or downhill running) and the post-competition analysis of some test, for example marathon.

This type of exercise called eccentric exercise (rapid stretching of the muscle by contraction) is successfully included in the training of different sport contexts improving strength, muscular power, coordination and performance through the improvement in the recruitment of motor units, a increase in reflex enhancement and changes in the elastic properties of muscle and connective tissue. However, by containing an eccentric exercise phase, it is highly associated with muscle damage and pain.

As older people appear to be more susceptible to exercise-induced muscle damage than younger adults, older people with a genetic predisposition to greater muscle damage, may be at a greater risk of developing muscle–tendon unit injury.

REGENERATION AFTER MUSCLE DAMAGE

Satellite cells are mononuclear cells that live outside of differentiated muscles cells. They have their own membrane but share the basement membrane with the muscle fibers. For tissue recovery an essential factor is that the basement membrane is not damaged. These cells do not divide, except when they are required to replace dead muscle fibers. When they do, one of the "sisters" remains as a satellite, and the others differ, acquiring a narrow neuronal control and the ability to form sarcomeres (the contractile structures of muscle fibers).

During inflammation, in damaged cells the leukocytes generate peptides called "competence factors". When these factors are released, the satellite cells pass from the G0 or inactive state to the activity state (G1).
386.technical_description:
The regeneration of skeletal muscle is a complex process mediated by so-called satellite cells in which various factors are activated to repair the injury to the muscle tissue. Satellite cells are muscle stem cells located on the surface of the muscle fibre. Satellite cells remain quiescent until a muscle injury occurs. Within 24-48 hours of injury, these cells proliferate and migrate to the site of damage, where they differentiate into adult cells (myocytes) and fuse with other cells to generate new muscle fibrils to replace the damaged ones.

For this tissue regeneration to take place, there must be an inflammatory signal that activates the satellite cells. There are studies that relate genetic polymorphisms in certain genes to this inflammatory signal and therefore to a greater or lesser capacity for muscle remodelling.

One such polymorphism is found in the superoxide dismutase 2 gene or SOD2 gene. Strenuous exercise results in oxidative stress that causes damage to muscle fibres and an inflammatory response. The SOD2 protein has an antioxidant function protecting muscle from free radicals and facilitating muscle regeneration. Lack of functionality of this protein can cause free radical accumulation and cell death of myofibrils. The SNP c.47 G>A or Ala16Val (also coded as rs4880 in clinical databases) is associated with difficulties in muscle fibril repair. The A allele is associated with low superoxide dismutase efficiency under oxidative stress. Akimoto et al. in 2010 showed that runners with the AA genotype developed high plasma creatine kinase (CK) levels (a marker of muscle damage) after running between 4-21 km. These results were also in line with studies conducted by Ahmetov et al. in 2014, where they showed that, in power athletes, the AA genotype was less represented compared to athletes in low-intensity muscular sport. The explanation they give for these results is that track and field athletes perform sport-specific movements that are accompanied by stress on the musculoskeletal system through repeated eccentric muscle contractions performed over long periods of time, leading to muscle damage. Therefore, the A allele could impair protection against oxidative stress due to reduced efficacy of the mitochondrial superoxide dismutase gene. This may indicate that there is a link between this SNP and the level of athletic performance in sports with a potential risk of muscle damage.

Another SNP associated with exercise-induced muscle damage regeneration is the c.826C>T (p.Arg276Trp) polymorphism in the SLC30A8 gene (codenamed rs13266634 in clinical databases).

The SLC30A8 gene is associated with insulin response and the c.826C>T (p.Arg276Trp) polymorphism in this gene has been associated with low insulin secretion in response to glucose uptake. In recent years, several studies have shown insulin resistance and loss of muscle function not only in individuals with diabetes but also in healthy individuals. This insulin resistance is associated with reduced muscle strength and muscle mass, and the SNP c.826C>T (p.Arg276Trp) of the SLC30A8 gene appears to be involved in this defect. Exercise-induced muscle damage and the ability to repair it is negatively affected by the inability to synthesise glycogen in muscle fibres (due to an inability of the muscle to take up glucose).

Sprouse et al. in 2014 demonstrated that individuals carrying the TT genotype of the polymorphism were associated with fewer biomarkers of muscle damage following eccentric strenuous exercise in the elbow flexor muscles (less pain, less loss of muscle strength and lower plasma creatine kinase CK levels). However, carriers of the C allele of this SNP required more time to recover from strenuous exercise. Although these studies shed light on the genetic role of SLC30A8 in muscle damage remodelling, further studies are needed to investigate whether SLC30A8 genotype-dependent insulin production is associated with adaptation to endurance exercise and muscle protein synthesis.

442.name: Histamine intolerance
442.description: Histaminosis or excess histamine in the blood occurs when there is a high intake of histamine in our diet or when it is not metabolized and eliminated correctly.
442.overview:
Histamine is a substance that is involved in numerous biological and physiological processes. It is synthesised and stored in granules inside certain blood cells (basophils and mast cells) that are part of the immune system and is also present in many foods either naturally or as a result of the action of micro-organisms.

At a physiological level, histamine participates in the stimulation of gastric juice secretion, inflammation, neurotransmission, smooth muscle contraction, haematopoiesis, vasodilation and cytokine production, among other processes.

When there is a deficit in the enzyme DAO at intestinal level, histamine is not eliminated correctly and is absorbed in the intestine through which when it reaches the blood it can accumulate in different tissues producing symptoms similar to an allergic reaction, such as headache, hives, diarrhoea and constipation.

It is known that there are certain polymorphisms in the AOC1 gene that can reduce the activity of the enzyme diamine oxidase or DAO. In addition, there is another enzyme known as HNMT that also plays a relevant role in histamine metabolism. In the intestine, the action of DAO predominates and HNMT also participates, while in the brain HNMT is the main enzyme responsible for eliminating histamine at intracellular level.
442.technical_description:
Histamine is a secondary diamine produced by the decarboxylation of its precursor molecule, the amino acid histidine. Two main pathways of histamine metabolism are known: 1) Pathway of the enzyme diamino-oxidase or DAO, histamine is extracellularly degraded at the level of the epithelium of the small intestine. The DAO is produced by the apical enterocytes of the intestinal villi which secrete it during digestion and release it into the blood. 2) Through histamine-N-methyltransferase or HNMT, histamine is degraded intracellularly. HNMT is the predominant enzyme in histamine degradation at the brain level. The HNMT enzyme is also produced by enterocytes, however, in the intestine, the action of DAO is more pronounced.

A person is considered to have histamine intolerance when they are unable to degrade dietary histamine at the intestinal level due to reduced DAO and HNMT activities, which triggers the onset of symptoms once histamine accumulates in the blood and exerts its action on tissues that have receptors for histamine on the surface of their cells.

Histamine receptors are distributed in our body in the nervous system, the circulatory system, the intestinal tract, the respiratory system, and the skin. The wide distribution of the four types of histamine receptors (H1, H2, H3, and H4) means that the clinical manifestations of excessive accumulation of histamines are greatly diverse:

- gastrointestinal discomfort: diarrhea, meteorism, stomach pain, and cramps

- cardiovascular problems: hypotonia, hypertension, anaphylaxis, and arrhythmia.

- respiratory problems: nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, sneezing, bronchoconstriction, and dyspnoea.

- skin problems: hives, redness, and itching.

- central nervous system alterations: headache, nausea, vomiting, alteration of the heart rhythm, and excitement.

Histamine can be of exogenous origin, usually from the diet. Some foods contain histamine naturally or as a result of the action of micro-organisms during food storage/transport. On the other hand, histamine can be of endogenous origin (originated by our body) and is released when allergic reactions occur, in cases of stress and infections that trigger the release of intracellular granules accumulated in basophils and mast cells.

It is important to bear in mind that histamine intolerance may be temporary and reversible, either because there is a very high intake of histamine in the diet in people with a predisposition to developing it and it is not completely metabolized, or because there is inhibition of the enzyme DAO by the binding of substrates that compete with histamine. Substrates with an inhibitory effect on DAO include 1) biogenic amines (putrescine, cadaverine, and agamatine in fermented foods), 2) alcohol, and its metabolite acetaldehyde and 3) certain drugs.

It is estimated that approximately 20% of Europeans take a daily drug that inhibits DAO, significantly increasing the number of people who are sensitive to histamine. Among the active drug components that could inhibit the enzyme DAO are antibiotics as clavulanic acid, colistimethate, and cefuroxime, the antihypertensives verapamil and dihydralazine, the analgesic metamizole, the mucoactive acetylcysteine, the antidepressant amitriptyline, the suxamethonium muscle relaxant, the ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) and the thiamine (Vitamin B1) (Comas-Basté et al., 2020).

Histamine intolerance may also be associated with the presence of inflammatory intestinal diseases that alter the intestinal mucosa, such as Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, celiac disease, etc.

Intolerance to histamine from food is not easy to identify. A wide range of tests are used to determine whether a person has the intolerance, among which are: determination of the DAO and HNMT activity after administration of oral histamine, hydrogen exhaled test to discard lactose intolerance and fructose malabsorption, ELISA for IgA and ELISA for transglutaminase autoantibody detection (to exclude celiac disease), colonoscopy and study of genetic variants that increase predisposition to histaminosis (Manzotti et al., 2016).

Knowing the genetic predisposition to histamine intolerance can be helpful in determining the origin of the intolerance but it is not considered a diagnostic test and if you have any symptoms you should consult your doctor for a proper evaluation (Maintz and Novak, 2007)

For the treatment of histamine intolerance, diets low in histamine (together with reduction another foods rich in amines metabolised by DAOs) and the oral supplementation of DAO, are often used (Lackner et al., 2019; Schnedl et al., 2019). These actions must be taken following the recommendations of a physician.

Among the foods excluded in more than 60% of low-histamine diets are included cured and semicured cheese, oily fish, dry-fermented meat products, spinach, tomatoes, fermented cabbage or sauerkraut, citrus, strawberries, wine, and beer (Comas-Basté et al., 2020).

In general, investigations into the genes that might influence a person to develop histamine intolerance have focused mainly on the study of variations in the gene that codes for CAD, known as AOC1 (amine oxidase copper containing 1; previously known as ABP1) and the gene that produces HNMT (histamine N-methyltransferase), as well as the different genes that contain the information for the different histamine receptors.

The most relevant SNPs affecting the function of the enzyme DAO in Caucasians are rs10156191 (Thr16Met), rs1049742 (Ser332Phe), and especially rs1049793 (His645Asp) (Comas-Basté et al., 2020). Ayuso et al. showed that individuals who were carriers of one of these variants showed a slight reduction in enzymatic activity of DAO. They observed that the combination of two or more SNPs had approximately 40% less DAO activity (e.g. carriers of 16Met and 645Asp), suggesting that the combination of 645Asp with another mutation could have a greater effect on DAO activity than 645Asp alone.

Furthermore, it has been observed that the polymorphism rs10156191 (Thr16Met) increases the risk of developing a hypersensitivity response to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and that the risk is higher in carriers of two copies of 16Met (genotype TT) than in carriers of one copy (genotype CT) (Agúndez et al., 2012).

Another relevant SNP is rs2052129 (G4586T), which is in the AOC1 gene promoter region and has been linked to a decrease in AOC1 gene transcription and a possible decrease of the DAO activity (Maintz et al., 2011).

The deficit in DAO activity has been observed in 80% of migraine patients and there are several studies that indicate that variants in AOC1 such as rs10156191 and rs2052129 could be highly related to the risk of migraine, especially in Caucasian women (García-Martín et al., 2015; Izquierdo-Casas et al., 2018).

The genetic variants previously indicated in AOC1 are strongly related to DAO activity and its expression, however, by themselves do not explain why a person eventually develops histamine intolerance. As previously mentioned, it is known that other factors, such as the presence of intestinal diseases, alcohol consumption, and drugs that inhibit DAO activity may be determinants.

445.name: Genetic predisposition to peanut allergy
445.description: The development of food allergy, as a complex condition, is influenced by both genetics and environment, as well as genome-environment interactions, including epigenetics.

446.name: Celiac disease predisposition
446.description: Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder that can occur in genetically predisposed individuals, in whom the ingestion of gluten proteins present in wheat, barley and rye, or derivatives, causes damage to the villi of the small intestine.

448.name: Vitamin E levels
448.description: Vitamin E or tocopherol is a fat-soluble vitamin that functions as an antioxidant and is important for its role in anti-inflammatory processes, its inhibition of platelet aggregation and its immune-enhancing activity.
448.overview:
The term vitamin E encompasses 8 compounds, four tocopherols, and 4 tocotrienols, which occur naturally in foods of plant origin. Tocopherol is the main and most abundant form of vitamin E and can be alpha-tocopherol, beta-tocopherol, gamma-tocopherol, and delta-tocopherol. The gamma-tocopherol form is the most consumed among American populations, while the alpha-tocopherol form is the most consumed among Europeans.

Vitamin E is insoluble in water. On the contrary, it is fat-soluble, like vitamins A, D, and K. For this reason, the absorption of vitamin E depends on the absorption of lipids in the intestine, requiring pancreatic and biliary secretions. Once bound to the epithelial cells of the intestine, vitamin E is incorporated into chylomicrons, being secreted into the systemic circulation.

Vitamin E deficiency is usually secondary to disorders that hinder the absorption of vitamin E from fats, such as liver disorders, disorders of fat metabolism, and disorders of biliary secretion.

Vitamin E deficiency due to low dietary intake is rare in developed countries.

Causes

Among the most common causes that can lead to vitamin E deficiency is impaired absorption of fatty acids involved in vitamin E metabolism, mutations in the tocopherol transfer protein, patients with cystic fibrosis (a lung disorder that can also affect biliary secretion), patients with intestinal and/or liver syndromes or diseases such as cholestasis or primary biliary cirrhosis, and those with some inherited monogenic diseases directly or indirectly related to vitamin E, such as abetalipoproteinemia (a digestive disorder characterized by malabsorption of fats).

Role of genetics

There are different genes related to vitamin E levels and evaluated by our genetic experts, including CD36 (rs1761667 polymorphism), SCARB1 (rs11057830 and re5888 polymorphisms), TTPA (rs6994076 polymorphism), CYP4F2 (rs2108622 polymorphism), and ZPR1 (rs964184 polymorphism), all of them involved in the different processes of vitamin E metabolization and utilization.

Prevention of vitamin E deficiency

Nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils are among the best sources of alpha-tocopherol, and green leafy vegetables and fortified cereals contain significant amounts. Most of the vitamin E in American diets is in the form of gamma-tocopherol from soybean, canola, corn, and other vegetable oils.

Supplementation of non-dietary vitamin E is only suitable for those with nutritional deficiencies, as the consumption of high amounts of vitamin E can cause metabolic and cellular changes in humans, if the threshold value is exceeded, and can be a risk factor for mortality.

245.name: Bitter taste perception
245.description: Sensitivity to bitter taste plays an important role in regulating the intake of some toxic substances in food, which could otherwise produce intoxication.
245.overview:
Taste is the sensation that certain bodies produce on the taste receptor cells. It is mainly determined by chemical reactions detected by taste (tongue) as well as by olfaction (smell). Eighty percent of what is detected as taste comes from the olfactory sensation.. That is why when a person has an upper repertory tract infection the sensation of any type of taste diminishes.

Taste is detected by the tongue and is produced by the taste buds which are able to distinguish among a large variety of tastes: sweetness, sourness, saltiness, bitterness and, since 2010, umami , identified and catalogued by the scientific community as a new taste. (Umami is a difficult taste to describe; it is associated with Orientals foods, especially with those high in monosodium glutamate.)

Specific taste buds are located on certain parts of the tongue. For example, bitter tastes are perceived especially in the lateral posterior part of the tongue, and sweetness in the anterior part.

According to Oxford Dictionary, bitterness is defined as a "sharpness of taste; lack of sweetness". It is a characteristic taste of bile, quinine and other alkaloids that produces a disagreeable and lasting sensation when it is especially intense. There are some thoughts that suggest that bitterness is perceived as such as a defense mechanism.

Researchers at the Howard Hughes Medical Institute (HHMI) at the University of California, and at the National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research (NIDCR) have identified a new gene family which codes the proteins that affect bitter taste perception.

Many medicines, such as aspirin, quinine and some antibiotics are bitter. In foods, bitter is often associated with coffee, chocolate, bitter melon, beer, olives, peels of citrus fruits, eggplant, lemon juice and grapefruit. Occasionally, bitter flavoring is used in cocktails.
245.technical_description:
There is great variability among people regarding the ability to perceive bitter taste, and these differences are likely to be greatly influenced by genetic variations or polymorphisms. It is known that about 25% of people are not able to taste the chemicals substances that are responsible for the bitter taste (such as phenylthiocarbamide or PTC and propylthiouracil or PROP); these substances are responsible for the bitter taste of many foods and beverages, such as cabbage, broccoli, coffee, tonic and dark beers.

There are 3 genetic polymorphisms or SNPs that are the most studied in relation to the ability to perceive the bitter taste in food (cabbage and broccoli) or drinks (coffee and dark beers). These three polymorphisms are rs10246939, rs1726866 and rs713598. Taking into account various scientific studies, it has been determined that to be able to perceive the bitter taste in a significant degree, a person must be a carrier of at least one of the following alleles in at least one of the 3 polymorphisms, these alleles are: rs10246939-C, rs1726866-G or rs713598-C.

These 3 polymorphisms or genetic variants are located in the TAS2R38 gene. This gene codes for a bitter taste receptor, therefore, variations in its sequence can lead to a loss of function of this receptor and it can explain the variability in the ability to detect bitter taste among people

417.name: Antioxidant capacity
417.description: The body's antioxidant defense system, especially the enzymes glutathione peroxidase and catalase, are responsible for keeping the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) low to avoid a physiological state of oxidative stress.
417.overview:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydrogen peroxide, are constantly generated in our body from cell metabolism and multiple exposure processes causing DNA lessions that may lead to malignant transformation.

Antioxidant mechanisms protect cells against oxidative damage induced by ROS. The first line of defence against ROS are the antioxidant enzymes manganese superoxide dismutase (SOD2), glutathione peroxidase 1 (GPX1) and catalase (CAT), which detoxify the superoxide anion and hydrogen peroxide. Their activity and ability to protect cells and tissues from ROS and their damaging products are influenced by functional genetic polymorphisms in antioxidant genes.
417.technical_description:
The CAT gene codes for the enzyme catalase, which is responsible for the control of the most important endogenous enzymatic antioxidants. It catalyses the breakdown of 2 molecules of hydrogen peroxide into 1 molecule of oxygen and 2 molecules of water and is thus responsible for hydrogen peroxide concentrations. Hydrogen peroxides are reactive oxygen species (ROS) that can induce oxidative stress and cell damage. Polymorphisms in the CAT gene, such as rs1001179, influence its enzymatic activity and thus contribute substantially to the development of diseases such as obesity, encephalopathies, renal failure, diabetes, cervical cancer and prostate cancer.

The T allele of the rs1001179 polymorphism in the CAT gene has been associated with reduced catalase enzyme activity, and thus increased levels of ROS. Bastaki et al. showed that the rs1001179 TT genotype was associated with decreased catalase enzyme activity, which is consistent with other studies in breast cancer patients in which the rs1001179 CC genotype had a 17% reduction in breast cancer risk compared to those with at least one T allele). Ahn et al. also showed that this variation decreases catalase activity in a dose-dependent pattern and found evidence that catalase activity can be modified by diet. In particular, they observed a reduction in catalase activity with increasing fruit and vegetable consumption, especially in women carrying the T allele. Other environmental factors, such as drug intake, may modify catalase activity and should be evaluated.

The enzyme glutathione peroxidase (GPx-1) is part of the cellular antioxidant system, is a selenium-dependent enzyme and up to 6 isoforms have been described in humans. All catalyse the reduction of peroxides and lipoperoxides using glutathione as a substrate. Disruption of GPx-1 activity leads to increased levels of peroxides and lipoperoxides (inducers of oxidative stress), which can be fatal to the cell and even more so to the organism, so this disruption is implicated in a number of diseases and physiological processes.

The main function of the GPx-1 enzyme is to protect haemoglobin red cells from oxidative breakdown. Studies in various cell types, especially erythrocytes, have shown that GPx-1 is the main hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) antioxidant enzyme, given its higher affinity for this molecule compared to catalase. The GPX1 rs1050450-A (p.Pro198Leu) polymorphism leads to a substitution of proline by leucine, which is less active than its proline counterpart.

Bastaki et al. found that GPx-1 activity was lower in men with AA genotype than in men with GA or GG genotypes. Chen et al. then conducted a meta-analysis showing that the rs1050450-A allele can result in altered GPx-1 expression and activity, leading to decreased production of ROS. They found that people carrying the rs1050450-A polymorphism have an increased risk of cancer compared to those with the GG genotype.

Several studies suggest that the GPX1 rs1050450-A allele, like other catalase polymorphisms, may lead to a worse outcome in people with different obesity-derived phenotypes (Rupérez, 2014).

437.name: Food intake control
437.description: Overweight and obesity are a worldwide health problem and cause an abnormal or excessive accumulation of fat that can have negative effects on health. The increase in its prevalence globally is mainly due to the way we eat and our lifestyle habits. However, there are certain people who have a greater tendency to be overweight, possibly in part due to their genetics.
437.overview:
There is a worldwide epidemic of obesity across all age groups, it is estimated more than 50% of the global population will be overweight or obese by 2030.  Being overweight is a known risk factor for many chronic diseases, such as type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular events. Increasing food intake is an important contributor to the obesity epidemic among other factors such as age, sex, physical activity, alcohol and smoking.

There are several genes that have been related to obesity and energy intake. Most of them seem to participate in the feeding control like for example LEPR (leptin receptor gene), NP-Y (neuropeptide-Y gene), MC4R (melanocortin 4 receptor gene), ANKK1 (kinase domain containing 1 gene) and FTO (fat mass and obesity–associated gene).
437.technical_description:
Body weight and fat percentage and distribution are finely regulated by multiple hormones and neuropeptides. These include the hormone leptin and neuropeptide-Y (NP-Y), which are two of the most important regulators of appetite and energy homeostasis. Leptin decreases food intake by signalling satiety via the hypothalamus, is secreted by adipocytes and, to the extent that adipose tissue is present in the body, there is more leptin in the blood serum (Mutschler et al., 2013). In contrast, NP-Y is expressed in the brain and sympathetic nerves, increases food motivation and delays satiety. Alterations in the NP-Y signalling pathway in the central nervous system contribute to the development of obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular disease (Zain et al., 2015).

The leptin receptor gene (LEPR) is expressed in the central nervous system and other tissues, such as liver, pancreas, skeletal muscle and adipose tissue. The main function of the leptin receptor is to mediate the peripheral effects of leptin. Several studies indicate that the rs1137101 (Q223R or Gln223Arg) polymorphism, located in the LEPR gene, is associated with obesity and predicts a slight percentage of body weight and composition. It has been linked to an increased risk of diabetes and triglycerides in men. Polymorphisms in the LEPR gene have been associated with a tendency to frequent eating or "snacking" in obese women (de Krom et al., 2007).

The rs16147 polymorphism in the NP-Y gene involves the loss of a binding site for a transcriptional regulator so that lower amounts of the neuropeptide are expressed when the rs16147-C allele is present (Sommer et al., 2010). Mutschler et al. conducted a study showing that the rs16147 polymorphism is significantly associated with waist-to-hip ratio (WHR; considered a measure of obesity) and serum leptin levels. The authors showed that women with the rs16147 TT and CC genotypes had higher WHR values compared to women with the CT genotype. Olza et al. conducted a case-control study in which they showed that rs16147 increases body mass index (BMI) in homozygous children, i.e. children carrying two copies of the rs16147-T allele have higher BMI compared to children carrying one or two copies of the C allele. Other studies indicate that people with rs16147 CT and TT genotypes have an increased BMI compared to the CC genotype. As NP-Y leads to an increase in food intake as well as the number of meals per day, the increase in BMI value in T allele carriers can be attributed to an increase in NP-Y expression. This age-dependent effect has been shown to be more pronounced from the age of eight (Hohmann et al., 2012; Olza et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2008). However, the relationship between BMI and the rs16147 polymorphism in adults remains controversial (Mutschler et al., 2013).

Dopamine is a neurotransmitter whose levels correlate with pleasure from eating and drug use. Dopamine signalling plays a critical role in the striatum of the brain, an area involved in reward and satiety signalling. Substances that inhibit dopamine signalling increase appetite, energy intake and weight, while those that activate dopamine signalling reduce energy intake and lead to weight loss. Studies in rats show that obese rats have lower basal levels of dopamine and dopamine D2 receptors (encoded by the DRD2 gene) compared to lean rats, leading to a reduction in reward and satiety signalling after a meal, leading to overeating to compensate (Stice et al. 2008) The A allele of the rs1800497 polymorphism, also known as TaqI A1 allele, located in the ankyrin repeat domain and kinase domain containing 1 gene (ANKK1) adjacent to the DRD2 gene, is associated with a low density of dopamine D2 receptors (Roth et al., 2013), people carrying the rs1800497-A allele are less sensitive to the activation of dopamine-based reward circuits and this makes them more prone to overeating. Interestingly, people with the rs1800497 variant also show a lower response to errors and a greater tendency to display addictive behaviours (Roth et al., 2013; Stice et al., 2015).

Melanocortins are expressed in the hypothalamus and play an important role in appetite regulation. Stimulation of brain melanocortin results in decreased food intake and weight, and mutations in the melanocortin 4 receptor gene (MC4R) have been linked to weight gain. Interestingly, this gene is involved in the dopamine and serotonin pathways, suggesting a role in mood regulation, and it is hypothesised that this polymorphism may play a role in susceptibility to depression. The rs17782313-C allele in the MC4R gene has been associated with increased emotion-dependent food intake and increased cravings. The presence of the C allele is also associated with increased BMI, probably due to excessive food intake (Yilmaz et al., 2015).

Polymorphisms located in the gene related to fat quantity and obesity, known as FTO, are closely related to obesity and increased BMI. Scientific studies suggest that the increase in BMI may be due to increased energy intake or food intake. Feeding and fasting modulate the expression of the FTO gene through different areas of the brain involved in energy homeostasis and the amount of fat in the diet also influences its expression (Karra et al., 2013). Studies in different animal models indicate that loss of FTO gene function leads to the lean phenotype while overexpression of FTO increases food intake, body weight and fat mass producing heavier animals. The rs9930609 variant in the FTO gene results in the change from T to A. The rs9930609-A allele has been linked to increased consumption of carbohydrates and high-calorie foods in both children and adults. Several studies have shown that homozygous rs9930609 AA individuals eat more food and are also less satiated than individuals with the TT genotype (Cecil et al., 2008, Karra et al., 2013, Timpson et al., 2008).

440.name: Vitamin B12 levels
440.description: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is a water-soluble vitamin obtained from animal products such as red meat, dairy products and eggs. It is essential for DNA synthesis and cellular energy production.
440.overview:
Vitamin B12 also known as cyanocobalamin (cobalamin) is a member of the vitamin group B, essential one-carbon metabolism, in which folate transfers one-carbon groups in a range of biological processes including DNA and protein synthesis, methylation, and homocysteine metabolism. In these processes soluble B vitamins (folate, B6, and B12) act as cofactors or substrates.

Clinical B12 deficiency, with haematological or neurological manifestations, is relatively uncommon. Low or marginal B12 deficiency without haematological or neurological impairments is frequently caused by an inadequate diet, especially among children and women of reproductive age. Low B12 levels are more frequent among older adults (≥60 years of age) than young adults (19–39 years of age) and more prevalent in women than men.

The determination of methylmalonic acid (MMA), an indicator of the B12 status, and homocysteine are frequently tested in clinical practice to detect B12 deficiency (Green et al., 2017). Measurement of total serum B12 levels has the limitation of that it includes total circulating vitamin B12, of which approximately 80% is bound to haptocorrrin, and therefore, not bioavailable for cellular uptake. However, the determination of B12 levels in clinical practice is not obsolete since it can be helpful to predict the prognosis and status of several diseases that are related to high vitamin B12 levels such as cancer and autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.

It has been described genetic variants that may alter vitamin B12 levels by affecting the proteins involved in absorption, cellular uptake, and intracellular metabolism. According to studies conducted in monozygotic and dizygotic twins, the heritability of vitamin B12 levels is approximately 59%.
440.technical_description:
Methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) is a crucial enzyme involved in the folate metabolism pathway. MTHFR catalyses the reduction of 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate to 5-methyltetrahydrofolate in an irreversible reaction. The products of the folate pathway are involved in the transformation of homocysteine to methionine, an essential precursor for DNA methylation and gene regulation.

C677T (rs1801133) and A1298C (rs1801131) are two widely studied MTHFR polymorphisms that reduce enzymatic activity and an altered distribution of intracellular folate. However, their effect on B12 levels remains unclear and several studies in different populations have found no association with serum B12 levels, except in the case of individuals who are homozygous for the C677T polymorphism (AA genotype) where reduced intestinal absorption of vitamin B12 and reduced folate levels have been found (Surendran et al., 2018).

There are other polymorphisms in genes involved in folate and vitamin B12 metabolic processing that have been extensively studied, such as RFC1 (rs1051266), MTRR (rs162036; rs1801394; rs10380) and MTR (rs1805087), but there is insufficient evidence on their relationship with vitamin B12 levels.

The fucosyltransferase 2 (FUT2) gene encodes the secretory enzyme galactoside 2-α-L-fucosyltransferase 2 that transfers terminal fucose residues to mucin glucans that are expressed on mucosal surfaces and are involved in gut microbiota interaction.

Some genetic variants in FUT2, including rs601338 (p.Trp154Ter), are thought to result in the lack of terminal fucose residues on mucin glucans and people with the AA genotype are considered 'non-secretors'. Some authors suggest that the lack of fucose residues may affect the proper binding of the microbiota to the intestinal mucosa and may decrease susceptibility to Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) infection, probably leading to vitamin B12 malabsorption. However, recent studies indicate that p.Trp154Ter is not correlated with H. pylori infection.

The study by Velkova et al. showed that FUT2 exerts its effect on total blood vitamin B12 concentration via haptocorrin and not via holotranscobalamin. The "non-secretor" status determined by the rs601338 AA genotype appears to be associated with an increase in total vitamin B12 concentration that is 10-20% higher than that observed in secretors (GG genotype). No differences were found between secretors and non-secretors for holotranscobalamin levels which is consistent with FUT2 exerting its influence through its fucosylation function, haptocorrin is a glycosylated protein and transcobalamin is not. This suggests that FUT2 activity affects vitamin B12 recycling and not vitamin B12 absorption and assimilation (Velkova et al., 2017).

The fucosyltransferase 6 (FUT6) gene encodes a Golgi membrane protein involved in the synthesis of fucosylated glycans, which mediate the binding of microorganisms to the intestinal mucosa, therefore, one hypothesis for how FUT6 polymorphisms may affect B12 levels would be that they affect microbial interaction with the host. Another hypothesis is that genetic variants may disrupt the formation of fucosyltransferases that mediate the glycosylation of B12-binding proteins and their receptors, which would influence vitamin B12 concentrations.

Carriers of the rs3760776-A allele appear to have higher vitamin B12 levels in Chinese, Icelandic and Indian populations (Nongmaithem et al., 2017; Surendran et al., 2018). The SNP rs3760776 is located in the promoter region of the FUT6 gene and several studies suggest that it could affect transcription factor binding and FUT6 expression.

The rs41281112 polymorphism was first associated with vitamin B12 levels in the study of 1496 Chinese men by Lin et al. It is found in the CLYBL gene which codes for the mitochondrial citrate lyase-like protein or "citrate lyase beta like". Healthy individuals homozygous for CLYBL mutations such as rs41281112 (TT genotype), which result in a premature stop codon and loss of gene function, have been shown to have reduced circulating levels of vitamin B12. The frequency of the TT homozygote in the general population is 3%.

The role of the CLYBL enzyme in vitamin B12 metabolism is not well understood. Studies suggest that itaconate, a molecule produced by macrophages, converts coenzyme B12 (active form) into its inactive form, which in turn inactivates the MUT enzyme and ultimately leads to a reduction in serum vitamin B12 levels (Reid et al., 2017).

As indicated above, the TCN2 gene encodes for a key protein in the transport of vitamin B12 in plasma. Inherited mutations in TCN2 are associated with a rare disease known as severe megaloblastic anaemia and can trigger neurological problems that are very serious if not recognised and treated immediately with high doses of vitamin B12 (Green et al., 2017).

There are common polymorphisms in TCN2 that probably account for some of the differences in plasma vitamin B12 levels among healthy individuals. The most commonly studied SNP in Caucasians is rs180101198. In the meta-analysis published by Oussalah et al, researchers found that individuals with the rs1801198 GG genotype had significantly lower holotranscobalamin concentrations (57.486 pmol/L; CI: 37.900-77.072 pmol/L) than those with the CC genotype (71.135 pmol/L; CI: 50.274-91.997 pmol/L). It is thought that this could be because the rs1801198 GG homozygote encodes a protein with less affinity for vitamin B12 compared to carriers of rs1801198-C alleles (CG and CC genotypes).

Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase is a mitochondrial enzyme encoded by the MMUT gene (also known as MUT) that is involved in the breakdown of various proteins into amino acids and is dependent on vitamin B12, specifically the adenosylcobalamin or AdoCbl form. This reaction ultimately produces energy through the Krebs cycle.

Genetic variations in the MMUT gene have been linked to vitamin B12 status, such as rs1141321 or rs9473558. In a meta-analysis by Hazra et al. in Caucasians, it was shown that homozygotes for the rs1141321-T allele showed increased serum B12 levels compared to homozygotes for the major allele (CC genotype). Other studies corroborated this finding in Danish, Icelandic and Chinese populations (Grarup et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2012).

Mutations and polymorphisms in transport proteins such as haptocorrin expressed in the stomach, intrinsic factor, or IF, in the duodenum and transcobalamin II (TCN2) expressed in erythrocytes together with metabolic enzymes (e.g. MTHFR) have been associated with circulating vitamin B12 levels.

The uptake and transport of vitamin B12 into cells requires three major cobalamin-binding proteins and their cell membrane receptors. The TCN1 gene codes for one of these three main proteins, transcobalamin (also known as TCI, haptocorrin or "R binder"). During digestion, vitamin B12 or cobalamin is released from food and travels from the stomach to the duodenum bound to the transcobalamin protein. In the duodenum, it is released from transcobalamin by pancreatic proteases and binds to intrinsic factor until it reaches the distal ileum where it is absorbed. Transcobalamin 2 (TCN2) then binds to vitamin B12 to form holotranscobalamin and enters the body's cells through the process of receptor-mediated endocytosis.

It should be noted that measurements of serum B12 levels detect the sum of vitamin B12 bound to haptocorrin plus vitamin B12 bound to TCN2 or holotranscobalamin. Holotranscobalamin is considered the "active" form of vitamin B12 because it is the form that can enter cells, while vitamin B12 transported by transcobalamin or haptocorrin eventually returns to the lumen of the intestine.

Studies in different populations suggest that common polymorphisms in the TCN1 gene may affect serum B12 concentrations. Meta-analysis by Tanaka et al. showed that carriers of the rs526934-A allele have higher circulating vitamin B12 concentrations compared to rs526934-G carriers (β = 27.62; CI = 11.65-43.59; P-value = 1.51 x 10-6). Similar results have been obtained in studies in the Asian population (Lin et al., 2012).

510.name: Myoadenylate deaminase (AMPD1 gene)
510.description: Myoadenylate deaminase deficiency is one of the most common causes of metabolic myopathy. It affects 1 to 2% of the European population, although in most people it does not cause any symptoms.

248.name: Preference for sweets
248.description: Given the increase in cases of diabetes and obesity, it is important to know how intake regulation systems work and why the preference for sweet foods may be greater.
248.overview:
Sweet is one of the five basic flavors and one of the only ones that is universally accepted by all cultures and ethnicities as one of the pleasurable flavors. It is detected mainly in the taste buds on the tip of the tongue. Foods that have a high number of carbohydrates are perceived as sweet and artificial flavors that sweeten are called sweeteners. Sweet foods usually form part of the human diet as desert and breakfast foods.

The majority of our preferences for flavors is not biologically determined, but rather they are ead to sdifferences in how we perceive flavors associated with a type of experience. Although there are certain genetic factors that

It is more normal that the similarities in a preference for flavors reflect similar experiences with distinct types of flavors and foods. The configuration of flavor preference begins in the uterus and continues for the rest of our life.
248.technical_description:
Certain people feel a certain preference for sweets and a greater tendency to consume this kind of foods. It is currently known that part of this preference for sweet foods is determined by genetics, and in particular by polymorphisms or genetic variants located at the SLC2A2 gene, also called GLUT2 (glucose transporter 2). This gene seems to play a function of “glucose sensor” in the brain (in addition to its well-known transport function of the sugar inside the brain).

Scientific studies have found an association between the rs5400 polymorphism located in this gene (SLC2A2 or GLUT2) and increased sugar consumption. Specifically, it seems that individuals with at least one copy of the A allele for this polymorphism consume more sugary foods in a frequently way than individuals with GG genotype (who present a normal or typical consumption).

This genetic variation is also sometimes named by the amino acid change that is produced in the SLC2A2 (GLUT2) protein, which is a change of a threonine (Thr) amino acid to an isoleucine (Ile) amino acid at position 110 of the protein (p.Thr110Ile). The A allele corresponds to the Ile while the G allele corresponds to the Thr, so Ile/Ile or Ile/Thr individuals tend to consume more sugar than Thr/Thr individuals

438.name: Long-chain omega fatty acids levels
438.description: Omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids are the main long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids. They cannot be synthesized in our body and have multiple functions, although the most recognized is their involvement in immune system processes, where one has pro-inflammatory activity while the other has anti-inflammatory activity.
438.overview:
Long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids or LC-PUFAs are fatty acids that typically comprise 20 or more carbon and two or more double bonds that are divided into two groups: omega-3 and omega-6 depending on where the first double bond is placed in the molecule.

We can obtain polyunsaturated fatty acids from the diet as “essential fatty acids” that are precursors of the LC-PUFAs and contain 18 carbons.  We can find these in plant-derived foods as alpha-linolenic acid or ALA (18:3 n-3) and linoleic acid or LA (18:2 n-6) and our body must process both following two biosynthetic pathways to obtain LC-PUFAs omega-3 and omega-6 respectively.

On the other hand, we can obtain LC-PUFAs directly from seafood-derived products, especially cold-water fish, that are rich in long-chain omega-3 PUFAs as eicosapentaenoic acid or EPA (20:5 n-3) and docosahexaenoic acid or DHA (22:6 n-3). Processed omega-6 PUFAs are also present in animal products such as eggs and poultry.

LC-PUFAs serve to constitute other substances that have important roles in blood pressure and inflammatory responses, among other processes.

The key substrates LA and ALA are transformed by the same conversion enzymes, thus both biosynthetic pathways compete to produce long-chain omega-3 (EPA and DHA) and long-chain omega-6 (AA or arachidonic acid).

The modern western diet has dramatically changed in terms of the nutritional content of fats following the recommendations of replacing foods rich in saturated fatty acids with those rich in polyunsaturated fatty acids with the aim of reducing total serum cholesterol and LDL lipoproteins and, therefore, the risk of cardiovascular diseases. However, the modern diet is richer in fatty acids from soybean, corn, and canola oils, as well as in margarine and shortening that are rich in omega-6 and poor in omega-3. The increase in omega-6 consumption, in turn, translates into a greater inhibition of the production of omega-3 synthesized by our body that, if not compensated with the intake of omega-3, can have an impact on our health since it has been shown that long-chain omega-3 fatty acids have crucial roles in:

- Maintenance plasma membrane function. DHA is the most abundant fatty acid in complex lipids constituting approximately 50% of the weight of neurons membranes. Thus, it is important for neurotransmission, neurogenesis, and signal transduction affecting brain development and function.

- Energy production through the beta-oxidation process.

- Act as precursors of other metabolites that are present in practically of cells and organs of the body and participate in a wide variety of processes such as inflammation. These substances can have opposite effects, for example, AA derivates act as local hormones leading to acute and chronic inflammation while EPA, DPA, and DHA derived metabolites have anti-inflammatory properties.

World Health Organization recommends daily personal consumption of 0.5-1.0 g of EPA + DHA.

Recent studies have found polymorphisms in the FADS (fatty acid desaturase) gene cluster, which is strongly associated with our body capacity to convert the essential fatty acids LA and ALA into LC-PUFAs that can explain, in part, the inter-individual differences in blood and tissue PUFAs levels. It is suggested that these genetic variants may have an adaptative role in human populations that have moved to more vegetarian diets with a low intake of LC-PUFAs.
438.technical_description:
Our body is capable of synthesizing long-chain omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids or LC-PUFAs such as eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA; 20:5 n-3) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA; 22:6 n-3) thanks to the metabolic omega-3 fatty acid pathway that needs the precursor alpha-linolenic acid (ALA; 18:3 n-3) which must be obtained from the diet.

In the omega-3 pathway are involved two essential enzymes delta-5-desaturase and delta-6-desaturase encoded by the FADS1 and FADS2 genes respectively that participate in desaturation reactions (double bond addition) that together with the elongation reaction (addition of carbon atoms) transform the substrate ALA into the LC-PUFA EPA. After, EPA is transformed into docosapentaenoic acid (DPA; 22:5 n-3), and finally, docosahexaenoic acid (DHA; 22:6 n-3) is be produced from DPA (Chilton et al. 2017).

In the case of individuals with reduced or no seafood products present in the diet and vegetarians, the omega-3 pathway is the main source of long-chain omega-3 PUFAs and, despite this, low EPA and DHA status have been reported in these individuals. Therefore, gender seems to have an impact on long-chain omega 3 production. Women DHA production is superior to men, probably because women may facilitate the supply of DHA to their offspring which is essential for neurological development (Burdge et al. 2017).

Long-chain omega-6 fatty acids production requires the linoleic acid (LA; 18:2 n-6), a precursor, and that competes with the omega-3 pathway because uses the same enzymes to produce arachidonic acid (AA; 20:4 n-6). Metabolites derived from AA and EPA/DPA/DHA have opposite effects.  AA products act as local hormones leading to acute and chronic inflammation while the EPA, DPA, and DHA derived metabolites have anti-inflammatory properties.

Rs174547 is a single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) located in the FADS1 gene at chromosome 11 that encodes for the delta-5-desaturase and forms part of the FADS cluster constituted by three genes of the same family FADS1, FADS2, and FADS3 (Cribb et al. 2018; Lattka et al. 2010). It has been shown that minor alleles for SNPs in FADS genes cluster that are in strong linkage disequilibrium with rs174547 were related to reduced LC-PUFAs blood levels in Italian, European, and Chinese populations (Chilton et al., 2017).

Minor alleles in FADS cluster have been related to numerous phenotypes as cardiovascular diseases, cholesterol HDL and LDL levels, triglyceride levels, coronary artery disease, obesity, insulin resistance, among others, supporting the idea that genetic variation that alters LC-PUFA levels may play important roles in human diseases.

The rs174547-C allele was associated with higher levels of both precursors, ALA and LA (ALA is used to produce EPA and DHA in the omega-3 pathway, and LA is used to synthesize AA in the omega-6 pathway) and lower AA, EPA, and DHA concentrations. Multiple studies suggest that carriers of minor FADS alleles (including rs174547-C allele) may present lower desaturation activity and probably reduced FADS1 expression leading to a decrease in  AA, EPA, and DHA levels (Dumont et al. 2018; Lemaitre et al. 2011).

In recent years, some studies found a possible interaction between dietary intake of PUFAs and the body capacity to synthesize long-chain PUFAs. The rs174547 SNP is considered a strong candidate to assess these gene-diet interactions because it showed the strongest association with blood levels of LC-PUFAs. Dumont et al. meta-analysis performed in the French population confirmed that carriers of minor alleles in FADS genes were associated with higher concentrations of the substrates LA and ALA, low desaturase activity, and decreased AA, EPA, and DHA concentrations. This study found that LA dietary intake may modulate the effect of rs174547 on HDL-cholesterol concentration, waist circumference, and body mass index (BMI). In consumers of high LA levels, the presence of the minor C allele was associated with a reduction in HDL cholesterol levels. While,  for subjects with low LA intake, the C allele was related with a decrease of 1.58 cm in the waist circumference and a lower BMI in comparison to non-C allele carriers (TT genotype), the authors suggested that the rs174547-C allele in combination with low LA intake could result in low AA production that may protect against obesity.

It is accepted that the reduction in the intake of LA and AA together with an increase of omega-3 long-chain PUFAs would benefit most individuals. Furthermore, supplementation with LC-PUFAs is an emerging possible treatment to alleviate the symptoms or slow the cognitive decline in neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson and Alzheimer's and to treat people at high cardiovascular disease risk. Understand the genetic differences among individuals in the metabolism of dietary LA and ALA precursors will help us in the nearly future to elucidate why not all individuals will respond equally to omega-3 long-chain PUFAs supplementation and adopt personalized treatment strategies (Avallone et al., 2018; Siscovick et al., 2017).  Nevertheless, the contribution of PUFA synthesis to meeting the demands for LC-PUFAs of individuals especially with plant-based long-term diets is uncertain and further investigation is needed (Burdge, 2018).

255.name: Muscle endurance
255.description: Sports performance is influenced by multiple factors, with genetic factors determining between 20-80% of the variation in different traits such as cardiac performance, oxygen consumption and muscle fiber composition. It is the proportion in the two types of muscle fibers that determines whether we can have a greater or lesser facility to perform power or endurance physical activities.
255.overview:
Muscle endurance means the force a muscle, or group of muscles, must make to perform a repeated action against a resistance. To carry out several repetitions of an exercise is a form of muscle endurance the same as if you were running or swimming. If the muscles have to contract in a similar form more than once, it is muscle endurance.

Many factors contribute to muscle endurance such as strength, type of muscle fiber, training and diet. A larger, strong muscle can perform the same task many more times than a weak one. A large muscle also contains more glycogen or sugar for energy so that you can do a series or contractions or do work for longer periods of time.

Genetics of sport endurance

The distribution of muscle fiber is largely determined by individual genetics and is established just after birth. The percentage of Type I fiber in a sedentary middle aged person is between 45 – 55% (this percentage increases slightly in women). Sprinters tend to have more fast-contraction fibers in their legs while in long distance runners, there are more slow-contraction fibers. Biopsies of leg vastus medial muscle show that the proportion of fast fibers in shot-putters and jumpers and weightlifters can have up to 3 times more (60% fast fiber) than long distance runners (17% fast fiber) and a 50% more than body builders, for example (40% fast fiber).

Recent studies show the genetic susceptibility to distinct types of fiber and consequently, a greater ability for one sport or another. Therefore, there can be a genetic predisposition to be a sprinter (fast fiber) or an athlete in endurance sports (slow fiber).
255.technical_description:
Sports performance is influenced by multiple factors, with genetic factors determining between 20-80% of the variation in different traits such as cardiac performance, oxygen consumption and muscle fiber composition.

More than 150 genes are involved in different aspects of people's physical performance. One of the clearest associations is determined by the ACTN3 gene that is usually responsible for activating the different types of muscle fiber according to the sports to be performed.

The ACTN3 gene codes for the alpha-actinin-3 protein, which is only used in fast-contracting muscle fibers.

The polymorphism rs1815739 (c.1729C> T), also called p.Arg577Ter or p.R577X, is a genetic variation within the ACTN3 gene that consists of the substitution of an Arg (arginine) amino acid at position 577 of the alpha-actinin -3 by a protein synthesis stop signal (Ter or X premature termination signal). Presenting a T allele in this polymorphism (rs1815739 or c.1729C>T), means having a shorter and therefore less functional copy of the alpha-actinin-3 protein. Presenting two T alleles (TT genotype) means not having any functional copy of alpha-actinin-3.

Therefore, people with two copies of the rs1815739-T allele have a total lack of alpha-actinin-3 in their fast-contracting muscle fibers, being less likely to be sprinting athletes, and more likely to be endurance athletes. But it should be noted that this situation or feature is not pathological. On the other hand, heterozygous individuals have a functional copy of alpha-actinin-3, which is enough for the rapid contraction of muscle fibers to occur efficiently.

Another way to explain these results is to explain the mutation at the protein level (the genes code for proteins). The most common nucleotide for this rs1815739 polymorphism is the C allele. The C allele encodes for an arginine amino acid (Arg or R); while the alternative allele is the T allele, which codes for a signal of premature termination of actinine (Ter or X or stop codon). Therefore, said polymorphism or SNP is commonly known as R577X. Individuals with genotype XX completely lack expression of alpha-actinin-3, therefore, they have a lower tendency to be sprinters and therefore, these athletes tend to be more associated with endurance test.

Multiple studies have been conducted on muscle fibers and their relation with the ACTN3 gene. For example, a study conducted on a group of elite Australian athletes, determined that at least in women who presented an R allele (rs1815739-C), this condition was associated with sprinting athletes, while the X allele (rs1815739-T) was associated with endurance athletes.

509.name: Caffeine and sports performance
509.description: Caffeine acts on the central nervous system, the peripheral nervous system and skeletal muscle, which may have a beneficial effect on sports performance.

439.name: Levels of vitamin A (beta carotene)
439.description: Vitamin A is an umbrella term for several fat-soluble substances such as retinol, retinyl palmitate and beta-carotene. Its various metabolites are essential for vision, cell differentiation, epithelial barrier function and immune function.
439.overview:
Vitamin A has an important role in immune response, vision, embryonic development, and cell differentiation.  Because we cannot synthesize vitamin A we must obtain it from the diet as preformed vitamin A from animal sources or as provitamin A carotenoids that can be found in fruits and vegetables.

The most abundant provitamin A carotenoid in the human diet is beta-carotene. It is mainly found in yellow-orange and dark green fruits and vegetables such as squash, carrots, mango, papaya. Carotenoid content in food is generally not altered by different cooking methods except extreme heat.

Beta-carotenoids, and carotenoids in general act as antioxidant substances that protect the organism against reactive oxygen species or ROS and play an important role in the prevention of multiple conditions including cardiovascular disease and diabetes. There are studies that suggest that carotenes have a protective effect on non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and lung cancer.

High doses supplementation with beta-carotene (>30 mg/d) has been associated with a negative outcome and increased mortality in smokers. Beta-carotene dosing should be assessed by your doctor according to your individual needs and health status.

Vitamin A delivery from provitamin A depends on absorption and conversion efficiencies to one of the main active vitamins A metabolites as retinal, retinol, and retinoic acid. Interindividual differences have been observed in provitamin A carotenoids absorption and transformation to biologically active vitamin A metabolites. It is hypothesized that single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in genes that encode for enzymes involved in beta-carotene absorption and conversion can explain part of this variability.
439.technical_description:
Once we have ingested foods containing carotenoids (e.g. carrots, liver, butter ...), they must first be released from their food matrices or other elements and molecules that accompany them and then incorporated into the lipid drops and micelles in the intestinal lumen. This carotenoid transfer to these lipidic structures is conditioned by multiple factors such as food matrix, food processing, presence of fibers, lipids, etc. (Borel et al., 2017). Carotenoids are mainly accumulated in the liver, where they are transferred to lipoproteins for their release into the blood circulation and thus deposited and stored in several tissues such as the kidneys, adipose tissue, adrenal glands, testes, skin, and the prostate.

Beta-carotenoids levels depend on food intake and gender (is higher in females than in males). However, it seems that genetics contribute to the interindividual differences exercising its influence through single-nucleotide polymorphisms or SNPs in genes that encode for proteins and enzymes which participate in beta-carotenoid absorption and its conversion to vitamin A. Here we focus on the analysis of an SNP that can impact beta-carotenoid transformation to active vitamin A metabolites.

There are studies that found individuals who form only 9% vitamin A from beta-carotene (known as poor converters) compared with those who are classified as normal. These differences may in part be attributable to SNPs in the BCMO1 gene that produces the enzyme beta-carotene 15,15′-oxygenase, abundant in enterocytes of the intestinal mucosa. Such enzyme is essential in the regulation of lipid metabolism in the adipose tissue and liver and in embryo maintains the normal development under conditions of limited maternal vitamin A supply (Lietz et al., 2012).

In 2009, the genome-wide association study performed by Ferrucci et al. identified a relationship between the rs6564851 polymorphism and a significant increase in the fasting beta-carotenoid concentration in the Caucasian population. rs6564851 is in the promoter region of the BCMO1 gene and which encodes for the enzyme that participates in the transformation of beta-carotene into two retinal molecules (active vitamin A metabolites). They suggested that the rs6564851-G allele that causes reduced BCMO1 activity, may result in higher circulating levels of unconverted beta-carotene circulating levels.

Yabuta et al. study reported that higher circulating β-carotene levels were observed in  Japanese rs6564851 GG homozygotes. Females with the GG genotype have reduced 48% of the BCO1 activity, in other words, were not able to transform beta-carotenoid precursors to active forms of vitamin A (poor converters). Curiously, Yabuta et al. found that women with TG and TT genotypes significantly eat fewer fruits and vegetables than GG homozygotes and speculated that the metabolization of ingested beta-carotenes may play a role in controlling eating behaviour, according to this idea the GG poor metabolizers need to consume more fruits and vegetables to obtain enough vitamin A.
